1. Clarify the intended behavior by defining a two-step display model: show a summary first when a news title is selected, with an explicit "Read More" option that expands or navigates to the full report, satisfying both the summary-first constraint and the full-content access need.

2. Revise Requirement B to specify the context in which summaries apply (e.g., list/browse views or notification panels), while confirming that Requirement A governs the detail view — making full content available only after a deliberate user action such as clicking the title from a summary view.

3. Engage stakeholders to determine the primary use case and priority: if bandwidth or brevity is the concern behind Requirement B, update it to allow full content display on demand (e.g., behind a user preference or subscription tier), and align Requirement A accordingly to reflect that full reports are accessible under defined conditions.

Original_FR: Full news report is shown after choosing the corresponding news title.
Conflicting_FR: Only a summary of the news report shall be displayed instead of the full content.
Resolution:
1. Implement a two-step display approach: show a summary by default when a news title is selected, with a clearly visible "Read Full Article" button/link that allows users to expand or navigate to the full news report on demand, satisfying both the summary-first and full-content access requirements.

2. Clarify stakeholder intent and prioritize one requirement over the other based on context (e.g., mobile vs. desktop, user role, or bandwidth constraints), then formally deprecate or revise the conflicting requirement with documented justification and stakeholder sign-off.

3. Introduce a user preference or context-based rule: display only the summary in list/overview screens, while showing the full report when a user explicitly selects a specific news title from a detail view, thereby assigning each requirement to a distinct interaction context and eliminating the overlap.

Original_FR: Functionalities related to Electronic Programming Guide and DRM management shall be implemented in STB or media gateway and not in CNG.
Conflicting_FR: Functionalities related to EPG and DRM shall be implemented exclusively in CNG, not in STB or media gateway.
Resolution:
1. **Conduct a stakeholder alignment meeting** to determine the authoritative source of truth between the two requirements, identify which requirement reflects the current system architecture decision, and formally deprecate or revise the conflicting one with documented rationale and sign-off from all relevant parties.

2. **Clarify and partition responsibilities** by decomposing EPG and DRM functionalities into sub-components (e.g., EPG data retrieval vs. EPG rendering, DRM key management vs. DRM decryption), then assign each sub-component to the appropriate device (STB, media gateway, or CNG) based on technical feasibility, performance, and security constraints — updating both requirements accordingly.

3. **Introduce a hybrid architecture requirement** that explicitly defines which specific aspects of EPG and DRM are handled by which component (STB/media gateway vs. CNG), replacing both conflicting requirements with a single, unambiguous requirement that eliminates the all-or-nothing contradiction while satisfying the underlying business and technical needs of each stakeholder group.

Original_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall include the designation of the heating or cooling unit and a flag to indicate whether that unit is to be turned on or off.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall exclude the flag indicating ON/OFF status and only include unit designation.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended design by convening a stakeholder review to determine whether the ON/OFF flag is functionally necessary for the heating/cooling unit control interface; then update one requirement to align with the agreed-upon decision, deprecating or removing the contradicting requirement.

2. Reconcile the two requirements by splitting the function into two distinct messages: one that carries only the unit designation (for identification/selection purposes) and a separate control message that carries the ON/OFF flag, thereby satisfying the intent of both requirements without direct contradiction.

3. Rewrite a single, authoritative version of the Generate_H/C_Request requirement that explicitly states which fields are mandatory (e.g., unit designation) and which are optional or context-dependent (e.g., ON/OFF flag), then retire both conflicting requirements and replace them with the new unified specification, ensuring traceability back to the original stakeholder needs.

Original_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall recognize the values for the requested heating or cooling unit and the ON or OFF status that is being requested. Since the interface to control the units has not been defined, the part of this process that will issue the signal is designated as TBD (to be determined).
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall not process any values until the interface is fully defined; signals shall not be issued for undefined units.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the TBD Scope**: Amend Requirement A to explicitly state that the recognition of heating/cooling values and ON/OFF status is implemented now, but the signal issuance is deferred (TBD) until the interface is defined. This aligns with Requirement B by confirming no signals are issued for undefined interfaces, while preserving the recognition logic already specified in Requirement A.

2. **Split into Two Phased Requirements**: Decompose the functionality into two distinct requirements — one covering the current scope (value recognition and status parsing, which can be implemented now) and a second requirement governing signal issuance (which is explicitly blocked until the interface is fully defined). This eliminates the ambiguity about what "processing" means and resolves the contradiction between recognizing values and withholding signal output.

3. **Add a Precondition/Guard Clause**: Update both requirements to include a shared precondition stating that signal issuance is gated on interface definition approval. Requirement A retains its recognition logic but adds a guard: *"Signal issuance shall only occur once the interface is formally defined and approved."* Requirement B is then satisfied as a natural consequence, removing the conflict by making interface readiness the explicit trigger for full processing.

Original_FR: geo-location feature to be able to find a user
Conflicting_FR: The geo-location feature shall not track any user location to protect privacy.
Resolution:
1. Implement an **opt-in, user-controlled location sharing** model where users explicitly consent to and actively enable location sharing each time they want to be found, ensuring location data is never passively tracked or stored without deliberate user action.

2. Replace continuous tracking with a **one-time, on-demand location disclosure** mechanism — a user manually shares their current location as a static snapshot (e.g., a shareable link or pin) that expires after a short time, satisfying the "find a user" need without persistent tracking.

3. Adopt a **proximity-based, anonymized matching approach** (e.g., using hashed or coarse location zones) where the system confirms two users are near each other without storing or exposing precise coordinates, balancing discoverability with privacy protection.

Original_FR: GPS Connection DESC: The application should be connected to the GPS device.
Conflicting_FR: The application shall function without GPS connection; GPS is optional.
Resolution:
1. Redefine Requirement A to specify that the application **supports** GPS connectivity as an optional enhancement, while Requirement B remains the baseline, ensuring the app functions fully without GPS but leverages it when available.

2. Introduce a **graceful degradation design**: specify that GPS connection is attempted at startup and used when available, but the application falls back to non-GPS mode automatically if no device is detected — satisfying both the integration intent of A and the independence requirement of B.

3. Clarify stakeholder intent by splitting functionality into **GPS-dependent features** (e.g., location tracking) and **GPS-independent features** (core functionality), documenting which features require GPS and which do not, effectively resolving the ambiguity between the two requirements.

Original_FR: Icons are according to the Android Icon Guidelines.
Conflicting_FR: Icons shall be customized arbitrarily and not follow Android Icon Guidelines.
Resolution:
1. Prioritize Requirement A and remove Requirement B, ensuring all icons comply with Android Icon Guidelines to maintain platform consistency, accessibility, and user familiarity — unless a specific, justified business case for custom icons is documented and approved.

2. Introduce a tiered approach: define a set of core/system icons that strictly follow Android Icon Guidelines (Requirement A), while allowing a controlled subset of branded or decorative icons to be customized (Requirement B) — clearly specifying which icons fall into each category.

3. Rewrite both requirements into a single, reconciled requirement such as: "Icons shall follow Android Icon Guidelines by default; deviations for custom branding purposes are permitted only where they do not violate accessibility, size, and contrast standards defined in the guidelines," thereby preserving platform compliance while allowing limited customization.

Original_FR: If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events.
Conflicting_FR: The UIMiddleware shall send notifications only for critical events; all other events are ignored.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a configurable notification filter that allows clients to specify their preferred event scope (all events vs. critical-only) at registration time, satisfying both use cases by making the behavior client-driven rather than system-wide.

2. Redefine the requirements by splitting responsibility: clarify that Requirement B defines the system default behavior (critical events only), while Requirement A defines an opt-in override available to clients who explicitly register for full event notifications, making both requirements complementary rather than conflicting.

3. Escalate to stakeholders to establish a single authoritative policy for notification scope, documenting the business rationale (e.g., performance, relevance, user experience) and then rewriting both requirements to consistently reflect the agreed-upon behavior, eliminating the ambiguity entirely.

Original_FR: If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications for specific events.
Conflicting_FR: The UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events regardless of registration preferences.
Resolution:
1. Adopt Requirement A's selective approach as the authoritative behavior: update Requirement B to explicitly state that notifications are sent only to clients who have registered for them, removing the "regardless of registration preferences" clause to eliminate the contradiction.

2. Introduce a tiered notification model: define two distinct notification channels — a "subscribed events" channel (honoring registration preferences per Requirement A) and a separate "system-wide broadcast" channel for critical/mandatory events (satisfying Requirement B's intent) — so both behaviors coexist with clear, non-conflicting scopes.

3. Clarify intent through stakeholder review and rewrite both requirements: if Requirement B was intended to ensure no events are silently dropped, restate it as "The UIMiddleware shall log or queue notifications for all events, but shall only deliver notifications to clients based on their registration preferences," reconciling both requirements under a unified, unambiguous policy.

Original_FR: If a command is issued by the VaadinUI for an UAV, the VaadinUI shall request control of that UAV.
Conflicting_FR: VaadinUI shall not request control of the UAV; commands are executed automatically by the UAV system.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system architecture by defining a clear separation of concerns: if the UAV system executes commands autonomously, rewrite Requirement A to specify that VaadinUI only submits command requests to the UAV system, which then handles control authorization and execution internally — eliminating the need for explicit control requests from the UI.

2. Introduce a conditional control model: revise the requirements so that VaadinUI requests control only when operating in "manual override mode," while Requirement B applies to the default "autonomous mode." This resolves the conflict by scoping each requirement to a distinct operational context.

3. Replace both conflicting requirements with a single unified requirement that clearly states the intended behavior, e.g., "When a command is issued via VaadinUI, the command shall be forwarded to the UAV system, which is solely responsible for authorization, control acquisition, and execution — VaadinUI shall have no role in requesting or managing UAV control." This eliminates ambiguity by consolidating the two contradictory statements into one authoritative rule.

Original_FR: If a flight plan is active and the "hover in place" command is rescinded, the FlightManager shall notify the UAV to continue to the originally designated waypoint.
Conflicting_FR: If the "hover in place" command is rescinded, the UAV shall remain hovering until manual override is performed.
Resolution:
1. **Prioritize flight plan continuity with explicit opt-out:** Revise Requirement B to clarify that automatic resumption of the active flight plan occurs when the "hover in place" command is rescinded, and that a manual override is only required to *prevent* resumption (e.g., "If the 'hover in place' command is rescinded and an active flight plan exists, the UAV shall resume navigation to the designated waypoint unless a manual override is issued within a defined timeout period").

2. **Introduce a context-dependent behavioral split:** Differentiate behavior based on flight plan status — Requirement A governs when an active flight plan exists (UAV resumes waypoint navigation), while Requirement B governs when no active flight plan exists (UAV remains hovering until manual override). This eliminates the overlap by making each requirement applicable to a mutually exclusive system state.

3. **Add an explicit operator confirmation step:** Revise both requirements to require an operator confirmation prompt when the "hover in place" command is rescinded. The FlightManager notifies the operator of the active flight plan and presents two options: resume to the designated waypoint or remain hovering pending manual override. This preserves both intents while placing the decision authority with the operator, resolving the behavioral contradiction.

Original_FR: If a flight route is assigned to a UAV which is currently hovering in place, the FlightManager shall assign the route and maintain the hover in place mode.
Conflicting_FR: When a flight route is assigned, the UAV shall immediately exit hover mode and follow the assigned route.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a priority-based override mechanism: Define a configurable "route assignment behavior" parameter that specifies whether a new route assignment should immediately override hover mode or queue the route for execution upon a manual or system-triggered confirmation, making the behavior explicit and unambiguous.

2. Reconcile the requirements by splitting into two distinct use cases: Require that the UAV exits hover mode and follows the route immediately **only** when the route is assigned by an authorized high-priority command, while maintaining hover mode when the route is pre-assigned or scheduled, thereby preserving the intent of both requirements in different operational contexts.

3. Rewrite the requirements with a clear state-transition rule: Specify that when a flight route is assigned to a hovering UAV, the FlightManager shall transition the UAV out of hover mode and begin executing the route after a defined acknowledgment step (e.g., operator confirmation or a short configurable delay), eliminating the contradiction by introducing an explicit transition condition that satisfies both safety (controlled hover exit) and responsiveness (route execution) goals.

Original_FR: If a lead is scored to the high range the system will supply the lead data and score to the enterprise system.
Conflicting_FR: High-range lead data shall not be transmitted to the enterprise system for confidentiality reasons.
Resolution:
1. Clarify and align stakeholder priorities by convening a joint review session with business and compliance stakeholders to determine whether lead transmission or confidentiality takes precedence, then formally update or retire one of the conflicting requirements to reflect the agreed-upon policy.

2. Introduce a role-based or consent-driven transmission mechanism: transmit high-range lead data to the enterprise system only when explicit authorization is granted (e.g., by a compliance officer or the lead themselves), satisfying both the business need for lead routing and the confidentiality constraint.

3. Implement a data anonymization or tokenization approach where high-range lead scores and non-sensitive metadata are transmitted to the enterprise system (fulfilling Requirement A's intent), while personally identifiable or confidential lead details are masked or excluded (addressing Requirement B's confidentiality concern).

Original_FR: If a question is not understood by our API, the system will send a text containing an example question after the text with the API response.
Conflicting_FR: If a question is not understood, the system shall not provide any example question in the response.
Resolution:
1. **Prioritize Requirement B and remove example questions entirely**: Eliminate the example question feature from the API response flow, ensuring the system only returns the API's "not understood" message without any additional content. Update Requirement A to align with this behavior.

2. **Make example questions optional/configurable**: Introduce a configuration flag or user preference setting that controls whether example questions are appended to "not understood" responses. This satisfies both intents by allowing contexts where examples are shown (per A) and contexts where they are suppressed (per B), with clear documentation of the default behavior.

3. **Clarify scope and reconcile intent with stakeholders**: Engage the requirement owners to determine if the conflict stems from different contexts (e.g., Requirement A applies to a specific API endpoint while Requirement B applies to a general UI response). If so, explicitly define the scope of each requirement to eliminate the overlap; if not, hold a prioritization session to select one behavior and formally deprecate the conflicting requirement.

Original_FR: If a question is not understood by our API, the system will send a text containing an example question after the text with the API response.
Conflicting_FR: If a question is not understood, the system shall not provide any example question.
Resolution:
1. Prioritize Requirement B and remove the example question feature entirely from Requirement A, updating the API's "not understood" response to only return a clarifying message or error text without any example questions.

2. Reconcile both requirements by introducing a user preference or configuration flag: example questions are only shown if the user has explicitly opted in, satisfying Requirement B's default behavior (no example questions) while preserving the intent of Requirement A as an optional feature.

3. Clarify the scope of each requirement with stakeholders to determine if they apply to different contexts (e.g., Requirement A applies to a developer-facing API response, while Requirement B applies to the end-user-facing interface), then rewrite both requirements with explicit scope boundaries to eliminate the overlap and contradiction.

Original_FR: If LO < = T < = UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Conflicting_FR: If LO < = T <  UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the boundary condition at T = UO by explicitly deciding whether the upper boundary is inclusive or exclusive, then update both requirements to use the same operator (either `<=` or `<`) so they are consistent and unambiguous.

2. Consult domain stakeholders (e.g., system engineers or safety analysts) to determine the intended behavior when T equals UO (i.e., should it be treated as "normal/in-range" or "out-of-range/alarm"), then consolidate the two requirements into a single, authoritative requirement reflecting that decision.

3. If both boundary behaviors are intentionally needed for different contexts or modes, split the requirements into clearly distinct use cases with explicit conditions (e.g., "inclusive upper bound mode" vs. "exclusive upper bound mode"), ensuring each requirement has a unique, non-overlapping scope to eliminate the overlap at T = UO.

Original_FR: If LO < = T < = UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Conflicting_FR: The temperature status shall be output no more than once per second, even if T fluctuates rapidly within limits.
Resolution:
1. Add a rate-limiting clause to Requirement A so that it reads: "If LO ≤ T ≤ UO, the process shall output the temperature status, subject to a maximum output rate of once per second," making the frequency constraint explicit within the triggering condition itself.

2. Introduce a debounce/sampling mechanism in the design: the system evaluates T against [LO, UO] continuously but only commits an output at the end of each one-second interval, ensuring Requirement A's conditional logic is honored while Requirement B's rate limit is enforced architecturally.

3. Clarify the intent of both requirements by splitting them into a detection requirement (A: trigger when LO ≤ T ≤ UO) and a transmission requirement (B: transmit the latest status at most once per second), explicitly stating that the most recent valid status is buffered and dispatched on the next permitted output cycle, resolving the ambiguity between "shall output" (immediate) and "no more than once per second" (rate-limited).

Original_FR: If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Conflicting_FR: If T > LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Resolution:
The conflict lies in the lower bound condition: Requirement A uses `T < LO` (temperature strictly below the lower limit), while Requirement B uses `T > LO` (temperature strictly above the lower limit), which is logically contradictory for the lower threshold check.

1. **Correct the lower bound operator in the unified requirement:** Clarify with stakeholders which condition is intended and consolidate into a single requirement. Most likely the correct logic is `T < LO OR T > UO`, meaning the limit is exceeded when temperature falls below the lower bound OR rises above the upper bound. Replace both requirements with: *"If T < LO or T > UO, then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded."*

2. **Review and fix the upper bound condition as well:** Note that both requirements use `UO < T` for the upper bound, which is equivalent to `T > UO` — this is consistent. Focus the correction solely on the lower bound, changing Requirement B's `T > LO` to `T < LO` to align with Requirement A, and retire Requirement B as a duplicate after the fix is confirmed.

3. **Introduce explicit boundary (inclusive) conditions:** If edge cases at exactly `LO` or `UO` matter, clarify whether the thresholds are inclusive or exclusive, and rewrite the unified requirement as: *"If T ≤ LO or T ≥ UO, then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded"* — ensuring no ambiguity remains at the boundary values.

Original_FR: If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Conflicting_FR: The THEMAS system shall recognize temperature limit exceedance only if it persists for more than 3 consecutive seconds, preventing false alerts.
Resolution:
1. Merge both requirements into a single requirement that incorporates the debounce logic: "If T < LO or UO < T, the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as a temperature limit exceedance only after the condition persists continuously for more than 3 consecutive seconds, preventing false alerts."

2. Retain Requirement A as the detection trigger condition and refine Requirement B to clarify it as an alerting/notification rule, explicitly distinguishing between internal detection (immediate, per Req A) and external alert generation (after 3 seconds, per Req B), ensuring both behaviors are preserved with clear separation of concerns.

3. Conduct a stakeholder review to determine the safety criticality of the application: if immediate response is safety-critical (e.g., hazardous environments), prioritize Requirement A and remove or relax the 3-second delay in Requirement B; if false-alert prevention is the primary concern, adopt the debounce approach from Requirement B and update Requirement A accordingly to align detection with the persistence condition.

Original_FR: If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Conflicting_FR: If that thermostat requires no more heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, there is a clear logical conflict: Requirement A triggers a "turn on" request when heating/cooling IS still needed, while Requirement B triggers a "turn on" request when heating/cooling is NO LONGER needed — both resulting in the same action ("turn that unit on") under contradictory conditions.

1. **Correct Requirement B's action to match its condition:** Since Requirement B describes the case where no more heating/cooling is needed, its action should be to submit a request to **turn the unit OFF**, not on. Revise it to: "If that thermostat requires no more heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit **off**."

2. **Consolidate into a single conditional requirement with explicit branching:** Merge both requirements into one clear rule: "If the thermostat still requires heating or cooling, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on; if the thermostat requires no further heating or cooling, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit off." This eliminates ambiguity and ensures mutually exclusive, exhaustive conditions.

3. **Review and clarify the original intent with stakeholders:** Engage the requirements authors to confirm the intended behavior for both conditions, as Requirement B may contain a typographical or logical error introduced during authoring. Document the agreed-upon correct behavior before finalizing either requirement to prevent implementation errors.

Original_FR: If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall submit the request only once per thermostat per heating/cooling cycle, and within 1 second of detecting the need.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, the potential conflict lies in the tension between Requirement A's implicit allowance for repeated submissions (re-checking and re-submitting if the unit "still needs" to be on) and Requirement B's restriction to submitting the request **only once** per cycle, while also imposing a **1-second timing constraint**.

Here are resolution suggestions:

1. **Clarify "still needs" as a cycle-boundary check only:** Amend Requirement A to specify that the re-evaluation of whether a unit is needed occurs only at the start of a new heating/cooling cycle, not continuously. This aligns with Requirement B's "once per cycle" constraint and removes ambiguity about repeated submissions within the same cycle.

2. **Introduce a state-tracking mechanism:** Add a shared requirement specifying that the process shall maintain a per-thermostat state flag indicating whether a request has already been submitted in the current cycle. Requirement A's submission shall only execute if this flag is unset, ensuring compliance with Requirement B's single-submission rule while preserving the intent of Requirement A.

3. **Merge and rewrite as a single unified requirement:** Combine both requirements into one: *"At the start of each heating/cooling cycle, if a thermostat requires a heating or cooling unit to be turned on, the process shall submit exactly one request to turn that unit on, within 1 second of detecting the need."* This eliminates the conflict by consolidating the trigger condition, frequency constraint, and timing constraint into a single, unambiguous statement.

Original_FR: If the business rules allow this transaction is to be aggregated the PSAM must determine if it has an aggregation record for the card issuer. If the PSAM does not have an aggregation record for the card issuer a new aggregation record for the card issuer must be created
Conflicting_FR: The PSAM shall not create new aggregation records under any circumstances; missing records shall be ignored.
Resolution:
1. Clarify scope and precedence by convening a stakeholder review to determine which requirement reflects the intended system behavior, then formally deprecate or amend the contradicting requirement with documented rationale and change control approval.

2. Introduce a configurable policy flag that controls aggregation record creation behavior, allowing the system to either create new records (satisfying Requirement A) or ignore missing records (satisfying Requirement B) depending on the deployment context or card issuer agreement, with the default behavior explicitly defined in a governing policy document.

3. Reconcile the requirements by rewriting them as a single unified requirement that specifies the exact conditions under which new aggregation records may or may not be created (e.g., creation is permitted only when explicitly authorized by a specific business rule or issuer configuration), thereby eliminating the absolute contradiction between the two statements.

Original_FR: If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC it shall support the AKA authentication method (TS 187 001 [8])
Conflicting_FR: If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC, it shall not support the AKA authentication method and shall use HTTP Digest instead.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the authoritative standard**: Review the governing specifications (e.g., TS 187 001) and organizational policies to determine which authentication method is mandated for ISIM/UICC-equipped CNDs, then update one requirement to align with the confirmed standard, eliminating the contradiction.

2. **Introduce a conditional/context-based rule**: If both methods are valid in different deployment scenarios, rewrite the requirements to specify distinct conditions under which each applies (e.g., "If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC and operates in network context X, it shall support AKA; if operating in context Y, it shall use HTTP Digest"), ensuring mutual exclusivity and no overlap.

3. **Escalate to stakeholders for a binding decision**: Convene a review with the relevant requirement owners, security architects, and standards representatives to formally decide on a single authentication approach for ISIM/UICC-equipped CNDs, document the rationale, and deprecate or remove the conflicting requirement to maintain a consistent and unambiguous specification baseline.

Original_FR: If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC it shall support the HTTP Digest authentication or the NBA
Conflicting_FR: If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC, it shall not support HTTP Digest authentication or the NBA.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the intended security policy** by consulting stakeholders to determine whether HTTP Digest authentication and NBA should be mandatory, optional, or prohibited when no ISIM/UICC is present, then rewrite a single authoritative requirement reflecting that decision (e.g., "If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC, it **shall/shall not** support HTTP Digest authentication or NBA").

2. **Introduce a conditional or context-based distinction** if both requirements reflect valid but different deployment scenarios (e.g., different device profiles or network environments). Split the requirement into two clearly scoped variants: one for scenarios where the authentication methods are permitted and one where they are prohibited, each tied to a specific condition or configuration parameter.

3. **Replace the conflicting requirements with a risk-based alternative** that specifies an approved fallback authentication mechanism for CNDs without ISIM/UICC (e.g., certificate-based authentication or OAuth 2.0), explicitly deprecating or prohibiting HTTP Digest and NBA in that context, thereby resolving the ambiguity by redirecting to a defined, agreed-upon solution.

Original_FR: If the DPU-SCUI FIFO is full when DPU-DPA attempts to enqueue its data product, DPU-DPA shall suspend event processing until room is available on the respective FIFO for the current data product. Incoming data may be dropped while suspended. An error shall be reported DPU-CCM.
Conflicting_FR: DPU-DPA shall continue processing events even if the FIFO is full, potentially overwriting incoming data.
Resolution:
1. **Adopt a configurable behavior policy**: Define a system-level configuration parameter that allows operators or system designers to select the FIFO-full handling strategy (suspend-and-wait vs. continue-and-overwrite) based on mission context, making one requirement the default and the other an optional mode with clearly documented trade-offs.

2. **Prioritize Requirement A and revise Requirement B**: Retain the suspend-and-report behavior from Requirement A as the authoritative requirement, since it includes explicit error reporting and data integrity protection. Revise Requirement B to align with this behavior, removing the overwrite clause, or explicitly scope Requirement B to a different operational mode (e.g., degraded/emergency mode) with clear activation conditions.

3. **Introduce a tiered response based on FIFO fill level**: Resolve the conflict by defining a graduated response — DPU-DPA continues processing when the FIFO reaches a warning threshold (partial fill), but suspends event processing and reports an error to DPU-CCM when the FIFO is completely full. This reconciles the intent of both requirements by allowing continued processing under near-full conditions while enforcing suspension only at the critical boundary.

Original_FR: if the item the user wants is available for exchange, the user can request the exchange of items that he/she has with the item he/she wants.
Conflicting_FR: Users shall not be allowed to request exchanges even if items are available.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope and context of each requirement by engaging stakeholders to determine whether Requirement B applies to a specific user role, system state, or time period (e.g., maintenance mode, unverified users), and update both requirements with explicit conditions to eliminate the overlap.

2. Prioritize one requirement over the other based on business rules: if exchange functionality is a core feature, revise Requirement B to specify the exact circumstances under which exchanges are blocked (e.g., suspended accounts, restricted item categories), making it a conditional exception to Requirement A rather than a blanket prohibition.

3. Merge the two requirements into a single, unified requirement that clearly defines the conditions under which exchange requests are permitted and prohibited (e.g., "Users may request item exchanges only when the desired item is available AND the user meets eligibility criteria X; otherwise, exchange requests shall be disallowed"), ensuring no contradictory statements remain.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the high average score then it will be process by the ARA functionality.
Conflicting_FR: Leads with high average score shall be ignored by the ARA functionality.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended behavior by convening a stakeholder review to determine the correct handling of high-average-score leads by ARA, then update one requirement to align with the agreed decision (either process or ignore), and deprecate the conflicting one.

2. Introduce a clear scoring tier definition document that distinguishes "high average score" from other score ranges, and rewrite both requirements using precise, unambiguous thresholds (e.g., "scores between X and Y") to eliminate terminological overlap and ensure each score range maps to exactly one ARA behavior.

3. If both intents are partially valid in different contexts, split the requirements by adding explicit conditions (e.g., lead source, campaign type, or business unit), so that high-average-score leads are processed by ARA under one defined context and ignored under another, removing the direct contradiction.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the low average then it will be returned to the supplying vendor
Conflicting_FR: Leads with low average score shall instead be processed by ARA functionality.
Resolution:
1. Clarify and unify the disposition rule by defining a single authoritative action for low-average-scored leads (either return to vendor OR process via ARA), documenting the business rationale, and retiring the conflicting requirement.

2. Introduce a conditional workflow that sequences both actions: low-average leads are first processed by ARA functionality, and only if ARA cannot resolve or qualify the lead is it then returned to the supplying vendor, making the two requirements complementary rather than contradictory.

3. Define distinct score sub-ranges within the "low average" band (e.g., "low-low" vs. "low-high") so that one sub-range triggers ARA processing and the other triggers return to the vendor, eliminating the overlap and giving each requirement a clear, non-conflicting scope.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the medium average then it will be set for manual verification by an Enrollment Coordinator through the eleads system.
Conflicting_FR: Leads with medium average score shall be automatically approved without verification.
Resolution:
1. Clarify and align stakeholder intent by convening a meeting with business owners and enrollment coordinators to determine the correct handling of medium-score leads, then update one requirement to eliminate the contradiction (either enforce manual verification or automatic approval, not both).

2. Introduce a sub-scoring threshold within the "medium average" range: leads in the lower-medium band are routed to manual verification by an Enrollment Coordinator, while leads in the upper-medium band are automatically approved — replacing both conflicting requirements with a single, tiered rule.

3. Deprecate Requirement B and retain Requirement A as the authoritative rule, documenting the decision rationale (e.g., risk mitigation, compliance) to ensure that medium-score leads always undergo manual verification through the eleads system, removing any ambiguity about automatic approval.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: Additional cooling requests shall be rejected instead of being queued.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended behavior by deciding on a single queuing strategy: either adopt Requirement B's rejection approach (removing the LIFO queue mechanism entirely) or retain Requirement A's queuing approach, then update both requirements to reflect the agreed-upon decision consistently.

2. Introduce a priority-based distinction: keep the LIFO queue (Requirement A) for high-priority or critical cooling requests, while applying the rejection policy (Requirement B) to standard or low-priority requests, explicitly defining the criteria that determine which category a request falls into.

3. Add a configurable system parameter that allows operators to select either "queue mode" (LIFO, per Requirement A) or "reject mode" (per Requirement B) depending on the operational context, and update both requirements to reference this configuration option so they are no longer mutually exclusive.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: Requests shall be queued only if the maximum number of units is not exceeded.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the queuing trigger condition by aligning both requirements to use the same threshold: requests should be queued **when** the maximum number of units **is already running** (i.e., the limit is reached, not exceeded), making Requirement B consistent with Requirement A's intent.

2. Rewrite Requirement B to explicitly state: "Requests shall be queued when the maximum number of units is currently running and no additional units can be started," removing the ambiguous phrase "not exceeded" and aligning it with the LIFO queuing behavior described in Requirement A.

3. Split the two concerns into separate, non-conflicting requirements: one defining the queuing condition (e.g., "A request shall be queued when the maximum number of running units has been reached") and another defining the queuing discipline (e.g., "Queued requests shall be managed using a LIFO strategy"), eliminating the implicit contradiction between "already running" and "not exceeded."

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: If the maximum number of  cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, the conflict lies in **scope**: Requirement A covers both heating AND cooling units under a single combined maximum, while Requirement B covers only cooling units with its own maximum threshold.

Here are resolution suggestions:

1. **Unify into a single requirement** that explicitly states whether the maximum limit applies to heating and cooling units *combined* or *separately*, e.g., "If the maximum number of heating or cooling units (tracked independently) is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue." This eliminates ambiguity about whether the caps are shared or distinct.

2. **Split into two separate, explicit requirements** — one for heating units and one for cooling units — each with its own clearly defined maximum threshold and LIFO queue behavior. This removes overlap and makes it clear that each system type is managed independently.

3. **Deprecate Requirement B** and extend Requirement A to explicitly address cooling-specific behavior, clarifying that the combined maximum governs both heating and cooling requests. If a separate cooling maximum is genuinely needed, document it as an additional constraint within Requirement A to avoid duplication and contradiction.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: The LIFO queue shall allow no more than 5 pending requests, rejecting additional requests until previous ones are processed.
Resolution:
The conflict here stems from Requirement A implying unlimited queuing (any overflow request gets queued) while Requirement B caps the queue at 5, meaning some requests get rejected rather than queued. Here are resolution suggestions:

1. **Clarify rejection behavior in Requirement A**: Amend Requirement A to explicitly acknowledge the queue capacity limit, e.g., "If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue *provided the queue has not reached its maximum capacity of 5 pending requests; otherwise, the request shall be rejected.*" This makes both requirements consistent and unambiguous.

2. **Define a unified rejection notification requirement**: Add a complementary requirement specifying that when a request is rejected due to the queue being full (per Requirement B), the system must notify the requester with an appropriate status message (e.g., "System at capacity, request denied"). This ensures the rejection behavior introduced by Requirement B is operationally handled and traceable back to Requirement A's flow.

3. **Separate queuing policy from queue capacity into a single coherent specification**: Merge both requirements into one authoritative requirement that covers the full lifecycle: "If the maximum number of units is running, requests shall be added to a LIFO queue. The queue shall hold a maximum of 5 pending requests; any request arriving when the queue is full shall be immediately rejected and logged." This eliminates ambiguity by consolidating the policy in one place.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is not running, this process will generate a request to turn on the requested unit.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall never generate a request to turn on a unit, even if the maximum is not reached.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope and intent of each requirement by engaging stakeholders to determine which requirement reflects the true system behavior; if Requirement B is a safety or override rule, explicitly document it as a higher-priority constraint that supersedes Requirement A under defined conditions.

2. Introduce a conditional exception mechanism: revise Requirement A to state that a turn-on request is generated only when the maximum is not reached **and** no system-wide inhibit condition is active, while redefining Requirement B to specify the precise circumstances (e.g., maintenance mode, emergency shutdown) under which request generation is prohibited, eliminating the absolute "never."

3. Remove or rewrite Requirement B if it was drafted in error or as a placeholder, replacing it with a more precise constraint (e.g., "The process shall not generate a turn-on request when a lockout condition is active or when the unit is flagged as unavailable"), ensuring it complements rather than contradicts Requirement A's normal operational logic.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select a few events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall select all events and ignore any limit on the number of events in the report.
Resolution:
The core conflict here is that Requirement A specifies selecting "a few events" (implying a limit), while Requirement B mandates selecting "all events" and explicitly ignoring any limit.

1. **Introduce a configurable report mode**: Add a parameter or user-selectable option (e.g., "Summary" vs. "Full") so that the operational history report can either retrieve a limited subset of events ("a few") or all events, reconciling both requirements under a unified, context-driven behavior.

2. **Rewrite Requirement A to remove the ambiguous limit**: Replace "a few events" with "the applicable events" or "all relevant events," aligning Requirement A with Requirement B's intent that no artificial cap is imposed, while retaining the sorting and ASCII file creation steps.

3. **Clarify scope and precedence between the two requirements**: Determine whether Requirement B is intended as a global override or applies only in a specific context (e.g., an audit or compliance mode). Document this explicitly — if B always overrides A, update A accordingly; if B applies only in certain conditions, add a conditional qualifier to B (e.g., "When in full-export mode, THEMAS shall select all events...") to eliminate the ambiguity.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select a few events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended scope by replacing both requirements with a single, unambiguous requirement that explicitly defines which events should be selected (e.g., "all events," "events within a specified date range," or "events matching defined filter criteria"), eliminating the contradiction between "all" and "a few."

2. Introduce a configurable selection parameter so the requirement reads: "If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select events from the event database based on user-defined criteria (e.g., date range, event type, or quantity limit), sort the selected events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file." This accommodates both full and partial event retrieval under one unified requirement.

3. Split the functionality into two distinct, named report types — a "Full Operational History Report" (selecting all events) and a "Summary Operational History Report" (selecting a filtered subset of events) — each with its own clearly scoped requirement, thereby removing the conflict while preserving both intended behaviors.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall only select a single event for the operational history report.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of the operational history report by redefining Requirement B to specify that a single event can be selected as a *filter or starting point*, while Requirement A governs the full report generation (e.g., "THEMAS shall allow the user to optionally select a single event to filter the operational history report, after which the system shall retrieve, sort, and generate the ASCII report file accordingly").

2. Separate the two requirements into distinct report types: redefine Requirement A as a "Full Operational History Report" that retrieves and sorts all events, and redefine Requirement B as a "Single Event Report" that retrieves only one specified event — ensuring each has a clear, non-overlapping use case and trigger condition.

3. Engage stakeholders to determine the intended behavior and resolve the ambiguity at the source — if the report should always include all events, remove or revise Requirement B; if single-event selection is a mandatory constraint, update Requirement A to reflect that only the selected event is retrieved, sorted, and written to the ASCII report file.

Original_FR: If the POS device supports multiple applications or multiple transaction types an interaction between the terminal and consumer or sales agent determines the CEP application and the function to be performed (cancel last purchase transaction)
Conflicting_FR: POS device shall always use the default application regardless of user interaction.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a configurable policy setting**: Allow the POS device to be configured in two modes — "default application mode" (enforcing Requirement B) and "multi-application selection mode" (enabling Requirement A) — so that deployment context (e.g., unattended kiosk vs. attended retail) determines which behavior applies, eliminating the absolute contradiction.

2. **Redefine "default application" as a fallback rule**: Revise Requirement B to specify that the default application is used only when no user interaction occurs within a defined timeout period or when the device operates in single-application mode, allowing Requirement A's interactive selection to take precedence when multiple applications are present and interaction is initiated.

3. **Clarify scope and precedence explicitly**: Amend both requirements to state that Requirement B applies to single-application/single-transaction-type devices, while Requirement A applies exclusively to devices configured with multiple applications or transaction types, ensuring the two requirements govern mutually exclusive device configurations rather than conflicting on the same scenario.

Original_FR: If the queue is not empty, this process shall remove one request from the LIFO queue and check the current state of the thermostat for which the queued request was made.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall remove requests randomly instead of checking thermostat state.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended dequeue strategy by holding a stakeholder review to determine whether the system requires deterministic LIFO ordering (Requirement A) or random selection (Requirement B), then update the requirements documentation to reflect a single, agreed-upon approach and remove the contradicting requirement.

2. Introduce a configurable processing mode that allows the system to switch between LIFO-with-state-check (Requirement A) and random-removal (Requirement B) based on a system parameter or operational context, ensuring both behaviors are explicitly scoped and do not overlap in the same execution path.

3. Rewrite the requirements to separate concerns: retain Requirement A as the primary dequeue mechanism (LIFO with thermostat state check) and reframe Requirement B as a fallback or load-balancing behavior triggered only under specific conditions (e.g., queue overload or timeout), thereby eliminating the direct conflict while preserving the intent of both requirements.

Original_FR: If the settings reflect a need for a heating or cooling unit to be turned on, the process shall issue a request to turn on the appropriate unit.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall not issue requests even if a unit needs to be turned on.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a conditional override mechanism: Rewrite the requirements to allow automatic requests by default (Requirement A), but permit a manual "silent mode" or "inhibit requests" flag that, when enabled, suppresses all requests (Requirement B), clearly defining the contexts in which each behavior applies.

2. Clarify scope and priority: Determine which requirement reflects the intended system behavior and deprecate or remove the conflicting one. If Requirement B represents a specific operational mode (e.g., maintenance or testing), explicitly scope it to that context (e.g., "During maintenance mode, the process shall not issue requests even if a unit needs to be turned on") rather than applying it globally.

3. Reconcile through stakeholder review: Escalate the conflict to the relevant stakeholders to determine the true system intent. Document the agreed-upon resolution as a single, unified requirement (e.g., "The process shall issue requests to turn on the appropriate unit when settings indicate a need, unless explicitly disabled by an authorized operator setting") and retire both conflicting requirements.

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a hit the product shall allow the offensive player to define a shot.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall prevent the offensive player from defining a shot even if the previous shot was a hit.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended game rule by consulting stakeholders to determine whether a hit should grant an additional shot (as implied by Requirement A) or not (as implied by Requirement B), then eliminate or rewrite the contradicting requirement accordingly.

2. Introduce a configurable game mode setting: allow Requirement A's behavior (hit grants another shot) as an optional rule variant, and Requirement B's behavior (no extra shot after a hit) as the default or alternative mode, clearly documenting both in the requirements.

3. Rewrite both requirements with explicit, mutually exclusive conditions — for example, define a single authoritative requirement such as: "After a hit, the offensive player shall [be allowed / not be allowed] to define another shot, depending on the agreed game ruleset" — removing the ambiguity and direct contradiction between the two original statements.

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a miss the product shall change the status of the defensive player to offensive
Conflicting_FR: The product shall keep the defensive player’s status unchanged even after a miss.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended game rule with stakeholders and eliminate one of the conflicting requirements, keeping only the one that aligns with the agreed-upon game logic (e.g., if a miss should trigger a role switch, retain Requirement A and remove Requirement B).

2. Introduce a configurable game mode setting that allows the behavior to be toggled: in one mode a miss triggers a status change (Requirement A), and in another mode the status remains unchanged (Requirement B), making both requirements valid under different contexts.

3. Refine the requirements by adding explicit conditions to distinguish when each rule applies (e.g., a miss in a specific game phase or scenario triggers a status change, while a miss under other conditions keeps the status unchanged), thereby removing the direct contradiction through scoped applicability.

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a miss the product shall change the status of the offensive player to defensive
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not change the offensive player’s status even after a miss.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended game rule with stakeholders and eliminate one of the conflicting requirements, keeping only the one that aligns with the agreed-upon gameplay logic (e.g., if the game follows standard turn-based rules, retain Requirement A and remove Requirement B).

2. Introduce a configurable game mode setting that allows both behaviors to coexist: in "standard mode" a miss triggers a status change to defensive (Requirement A), while in "continuous offense mode" the offensive player retains their status after a miss (Requirement B), making both requirements valid under different contexts.

3. Rewrite the requirements with a clearly defined scope and condition to remove ambiguity — for example, specify whether the status change applies only in certain game types, player levels, or rule sets, so that each requirement governs a distinct, non-overlapping scenario rather than contradicting each other.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall present months instead of dates for statistical report selection.
Resolution:
1. Replace Requirement A with a unified requirement that specifies THEMAS shall present the operator with a list of available **months** (rather than dates) for statistical report selection, making Requirement B the authoritative version and eliminating the contradiction.

2. Clarify the intended granularity by introducing a two-level selection hierarchy: THEMAS shall first present a list of available **months**, and upon selecting a month, optionally present the specific **dates** within that month — reconciling both requirements into a coherent drill-down workflow.

3. Engage stakeholders to determine the correct level of granularity (dates vs. months) based on actual reporting needs, then document a single, agreed-upon requirement that explicitly states the chosen time unit, and formally retire or supersede the conflicting requirement to prevent ambiguity during implementation.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present a list of available months instead of dates.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended granularity with stakeholders and update both requirements to consistently specify either "dates" or "months" as the time selection unit, eliminating the contradiction by retaining only one agreed-upon requirement.

2. Introduce a hierarchical selection approach: first present a list of available months, then upon selecting a month, present the specific available dates within that month — reconciling both requirements into a unified two-step workflow.

3. Make the time granularity configurable based on report type or user role: update the requirements to specify that some statistical reports use date-level selection while others use month-level selection, clearly defining which condition applies to each case.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Resolution:
1. Merge the two requirements into a single hierarchical selection requirement: the system shall first present the operator with a list of available months, and upon selecting a month, shall then present a list of available dates within that month, providing a two-level drill-down navigation.

2. Clarify the intended granularity with stakeholders and adopt a single unified requirement — either month-level or date-level selection — based on the actual reporting needs, removing the conflicting requirement entirely.

3. Rewrite the requirements to support both levels of selection as configurable options: the system shall present the operator with a list of available time periods (months or specific dates) from which to make a selection, with the default granularity defined by the report type or user preference.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall present specific dates instead of months for selection.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement A to align with Requirement B by replacing "months" with "specific dates," so the system presents a list of available specific dates for selection when a statistical report is chosen, eliminating the contradiction entirely.

2. Introduce a hierarchical selection approach: first present available months (per Requirement A), then upon selecting a month, drill down to present specific dates within that month (per Requirement B), satisfying both granularity needs in a two-step workflow.

3. Clarify the intended use context with stakeholders to determine which granularity (months vs. specific dates) is appropriate for the statistical report feature, then deprecate the conflicting requirement and update documentation to reflect a single, agreed-upon selection mechanism.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Resolution:
1. Unify the granularity by replacing both requirements with a single requirement that presents operators with a hierarchical date selection (first selecting a year/month, then drilling down to a specific date), satisfying both levels of specificity in one consistent workflow.

2. Clarify the intended scope with stakeholders and adopt one level of granularity: if monthly summaries are sufficient for statistical reports, keep Requirement A (months only) and remove Requirement B; if day-level detail is needed, keep Requirement B (dates) and remove Requirement A.

3. Differentiate the two requirements by context: specify that the system presents a list of available **months** when generating monthly aggregate statistical reports, and a list of available **dates** when generating daily/detailed statistical reports, making each requirement applicable to a distinct report sub-type.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present a list of available dates instead of months.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended granularity with stakeholders and update both requirements to consistently specify either "months" or "dates" as the selection unit, eliminating the contradiction by retaining only one agreed-upon requirement.

2. Introduce a hierarchical selection flow: first present a list of available months (Requirement A), and upon selecting a month, drill down to present available dates within that month (Requirement B) — merging both requirements into a two-step selection process.

3. Make the selection granularity configurable based on user role or report type, specifying that operators see a month-level selection for certain report contexts and a date-level selection for others, with each condition clearly defined to avoid overlap.

Original_FR: If the system detects a temperature limit has been exceeded, Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a continuous series of alternating 1000 Hz and 1500 Hz beeps on the supervisor???s computer.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a single continuous tone instead of alternating beeps.
Resolution:
1. Prioritize Requirement A and remove or revise Requirement B, as alternating tones (1000 Hz and 1500 Hz) are generally more effective for alerting supervisors than a single continuous tone, and Requirement A provides a more specific and safety-oriented behavior tied to a clear trigger condition.

2. Clarify the intended use case for each behavior and split them into distinct alarm scenarios — for example, retain alternating beeps (Requirement A) for critical temperature limit breaches, and assign the single continuous tone (Requirement B) to a different, lower-priority alarm condition, ensuring each has a unique trigger.

3. Convene a stakeholder review with both requirement authors to determine the authoritative alarm output specification, document the agreed-upon behavior in a single consolidated requirement, and formally deprecate the conflicting version to prevent ambiguity in implementation and testing.

Original_FR: If the system detects a temperature limit has been exceeded, Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a continuous series of alternating 1000 Hz and 1500 Hz beeps on the supervisor’s computer.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a single continuous tone instead of alternating beeps.
Resolution:
1. Prioritize Requirement A and remove or revise Requirement B to align with it, specifying that alternating 1000 Hz and 1500 Hz beeps are the standard output for temperature limit alarms, as the alternating pattern provides a more distinctive and recognizable alert signal.

2. Differentiate alarm behaviors by severity or type: retain alternating beeps (Requirement A) for critical temperature limit exceedances, and assign the single continuous tone (Requirement B) to a different alarm condition (e.g., a lower-priority warning), thereby eliminating the direct conflict while preserving both behaviors in context.

3. Convene a stakeholder review to determine the intended alarm output behavior, document the agreed-upon decision as the single authoritative requirement, and explicitly deprecate or delete the conflicting requirement to ensure a consistent, unambiguous specification for Generate_Alarm_Data.

Original_FR: If the system detects an invalid temperature, Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a continuous series of alternating 500 Hz and 700 Hz beeps on the supervisor?‘s computer.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_Alarm_Data shall output 1000 Hz constant tone instead of 500/700 Hz alternating signal.
Resolution:
1. **Prioritize and supersede**: Explicitly designate Requirement B as a formal update/override to Requirement A, retiring the 500/700 Hz alternating signal specification entirely and updating the requirements documentation to reflect only the 1000 Hz constant tone as the single authoritative alarm output for invalid temperature detection.

2. **Differentiate alarm scenarios**: Assign distinct audio signals to different alarm conditions — for example, retain the alternating 500/700 Hz beeps for invalid temperature alarms (Requirement A) and assign the 1000 Hz constant tone to a different, higher-severity alarm condition — thereby eliminating the overlap and giving each signal a unique, unambiguous meaning.

3. **Stakeholder review and decision**: Convene a requirements review meeting with relevant stakeholders (system engineers, safety officers, and end users) to determine the correct alarm behavior based on human factors, safety standards, and operational context, then formally document the agreed-upon single specification, trace the change, and close out the conflicting requirement with a documented rationale.

Original_FR: If the system detects an invalid temperature, Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a continuous series of alternating 500 Hz and 700 Hz beeps on the supervisor’s computer.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_Alarm_Data shall output no alarm signal for invalid temperature.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the definition of "invalid temperature"**: Determine whether both requirements refer to the same condition. If they address different scenarios (e.g., Requirement A refers to a sensor fault/out-of-range value while Requirement B refers to a legitimately undefined/null temperature reading), redefine and distinguish the triggering conditions so each requirement applies to a unique, non-overlapping case.

2. **Establish a priority/stakeholder decision**: Convene stakeholders (system architects, safety engineers, and end users) to decide which behavior is correct and intentional. If safety regulations mandate an alarm for invalid temperatures, deprecate Requirement B and update documentation accordingly. If silent failure is preferred, revise Requirement A to specify a different trigger condition (e.g., "out-of-range temperature" instead of "invalid temperature").

3. **Introduce a configurable alarm mode**: Replace both conflicting requirements with a single, unified requirement that allows the system to be configured per deployment context — e.g., "Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a configurable alarm response (audible alternating 500 Hz/700 Hz beeps or no signal) upon detection of an invalid temperature, based on a system-level configuration parameter set during installation." This resolves the conflict while accommodating different operational environments.

Original_FR: if the system unable to update the location , Show failure msg and return the user to home page.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall silently fail and remain on the current page without any message.
Resolution:
1. **Prioritize user transparency**: Adopt Requirement A's approach by displaying a failure message when a location update fails, but refine it to allow the user to stay on the current page (rather than redirecting to the home page), combining user awareness with minimal disruption.

2. **Clarify context-specific behavior**: Distinguish the scenarios in which each requirement applies — for example, show a failure message and redirect to the home page for critical location updates (Requirement A), while silently failing and remaining on the current page for non-critical or background location updates (Requirement B).

3. **Stakeholder alignment meeting**: Escalate the conflict to relevant stakeholders (product owner, UX team, and business analysts) to determine the intended user experience and system behavior, then formally deprecate one requirement or merge them into a single, unambiguous requirement that specifies exactly when and how failures should be communicated to the user.

Original_FR: If the user denies to use the location service , prompt the user to home screen
Conflicting_FR: The system shall ignore the denial and continue using location services without prompting.
Resolution:
1. **Prioritize user consent and legal compliance**: Remove Requirement B entirely, as ignoring a user's explicit denial of location permissions violates platform guidelines (iOS/Android) and privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA). Retain Requirement A and redirect users to the home screen when location access is denied.

2. **Introduce a graceful degradation path**: Modify both requirements so that if the user denies location services, the system informs the user which features will be unavailable, offers a limited-functionality mode that does not require location, and only redirects to the home screen if location is strictly mandatory for the app to function at all.

3. **Clarify stakeholder intent and rewrite requirements**: Facilitate a review session with stakeholders to determine the true business need behind Requirement B (e.g., concern about app usability without location). Rewrite the requirements so that the system respects the denial, prompts the user with a clear explanation of why location is needed, and provides an option to revisit permissions in device settings — replacing the conflicting directive to "ignore the denial" with a compliant, user-friendly alternative.

Original_FR: If there is a network issue, the chat window will display an error message.
Conflicting_FR: The chat window shall remain blank and show no message during a network issue.
Resolution:
1. Prioritize Requirement A and remove Requirement B, as displaying an error message during a network issue is the better user experience practice — it informs users of the problem rather than leaving them confused by a blank screen.

2. Clarify stakeholder intent by scheduling a review meeting with the requirement owners to determine the desired behavior; if a "clean" UI is preferred, redefine Requirement B to specify that a subtle, non-intrusive error indicator (e.g., a status icon or banner) is shown instead of a full error message, reconciling both concerns.

3. Introduce a conditional resolution: display a brief, dismissible error message overlay on the chat window when a network issue is detected (satisfying the need to inform the user per Requirement A), while keeping the underlying chat window content area blank and unchanged (partially satisfying Requirement B's intent of not cluttering the message history). Update both requirements to reflect this agreed-upon behavior.

Original_FR: If there is a network issue, the chat window will display an error message.
Conflicting_FR: The chat window shall remain blank during a network issue.
Resolution:
1. Prioritize Requirement A by displaying a clear error message in the chat window during a network issue, as it provides better user experience and transparency; formally deprecate Requirement B.
2. Reconcile both requirements by displaying a minimal, unobtrusive inline error indicator (e.g., a small icon or status bar message) rather than a full error message, so the chat window remains visually "blank" of chat content while still communicating the network issue to the user.
3. Clarify stakeholder intent through a review session to determine whether "blank" in Requirement B was intentional or an oversight; update the requirements documentation to reflect the agreed-upon behavior, ensuring only one authoritative requirement governs the chat window's response to network issues.

Original_FR: If there was an error here, then the unit failed to create a generic answer given a generic sentence. In this case, simply fallback to the error handling described in R4.4.2.1
Conflicting_FR: The system shall attempt to generate a generic answer even after an error occurs, ignoring fallback procedures.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify error handling priority**: Define a clear hierarchy where the system first attempts to generate a generic answer (per Requirement B), but if that attempt itself fails, it must invoke the R4.4.2.1 fallback procedure (per Requirement A). This makes both requirements complementary rather than contradictory.

2. **Introduce a retry limit**: Amend Requirement B to specify a maximum number of retry attempts for generating a generic answer after an error. Once the retry limit is exhausted, the system shall revert to the fallback error handling defined in R4.4.2.1, satisfying both the resilience intent of B and the safety net intent of A.

3. **Remove or revise Requirement B**: If the fallback procedure in R4.4.2.1 is a mandatory safety or compliance requirement, revise Requirement B to remove the clause "ignoring fallback procedures," replacing it with language that encourages best-effort generic answer generation while still permitting fallback when generation is not possible, eliminating the direct contradiction.

Original_FR: If user allows location service , create the user
Conflicting_FR: The system shall not create a user even if location service is allowed.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the business rule by consulting stakeholders to determine the definitive condition for user creation, then rewrite the requirements so only one authoritative requirement governs when a user account is created (e.g., "The system shall create a user only when [specific conditions beyond location service approval are met]").

2. Introduce a priority or dependency hierarchy by specifying that location service permission is a necessary but not sufficient condition for user creation, and define the additional criteria (e.g., verified email, accepted terms) that must also be satisfied, effectively superseding the blanket prohibition in Requirement B.

3. Separate the concerns by scoping each requirement to a distinct context or user type (e.g., Requirement A applies to standard users in a specific workflow, while Requirement B applies to guest or restricted accounts), ensuring the two requirements no longer overlap and each has a clearly defined, non-conflicting applicability.

Original_FR: If user within 1 mile of the campus , update location
Conflicting_FR: The system shall not update user location even if within 1 mile of campus.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user-controlled opt-in/opt-out setting that allows users to explicitly choose whether their location is updated when within 1 mile of campus, satisfying both privacy concerns (Requirement B) and location-tracking functionality (Requirement A).

2. Clarify stakeholder intent by reviewing the business context and priority of each requirement — if Requirement B is a privacy or compliance mandate, it should supersede Requirement A, and Requirement A should be revised to exclude the 1-mile campus zone or be removed entirely.

3. Redefine the scope of each requirement by specifying distinct conditions — for example, Requirement A applies only when the user is actively using a location-dependent feature and has granted explicit permission, while Requirement B serves as the default behavior, ensuring no passive or automatic location updates occur near campus.

Original_FR: In order to provide an indication of the status of the heating or cooling unit back to the requesting thermostat, Generate_H/C_Signal shall output the status information of the heating or cooling unit.
Conflicting_FR: In order to provide an indication of the status of the heating or cooling unit back to the requesting thermostat, Generate_H/C_Signal should output the status information of the heating unit.
Resolution:
