1. Unify the scope of the output by updating both requirements to explicitly state whether the status information covers the **heating unit only**, the **cooling unit only**, or **both heating and cooling units**, eliminating the ambiguity caused by Requirement B omitting the cooling unit.

2. Reconcile the obligation level by aligning the modal verbs: replace "should" in Requirement B with "shall" (or vice versa in Requirement A) so that both requirements carry the same binding strength, and then merge them into a single, authoritative requirement that clearly specifies the full scope (heating and/or cooling unit status).

3. Split the requirements into two distinct, non-conflicting requirements — one mandating (`shall`) that `Generate_H/C_Signal` outputs the status of the **heating unit**, and one mandating (`shall`) that it outputs the status of the **cooling unit** — thereby removing the overlap and ensuring complete, unambiguous coverage of both units.

Original_FR: In order to provide an indication of the status of the heating or cooling unit back to the requesting thermostat, Generate_H/C_Signal shall output the status information of the heating or cooling unit.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall output only the heating unit status, ignoring the cooling unit.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of Generate_H/C_Signal by convening a stakeholder review to determine whether the function must handle both heating and cooling units or only the heating unit, then update one requirement to align with the agreed-upon decision and deprecate the conflicting one.

2. Split the functionality into two separate functions — Generate_Heating_Signal and Generate_Cooling_Signal — each responsible for outputting the status of its respective unit, thereby eliminating the ambiguity and satisfying the intent of both requirements independently.

3. Revise Requirement B to be a constrained variant of Requirement A by specifying the condition under which only heating unit status is reported (e.g., when no cooling unit is present or configured), making it a conditional behavior rather than a blanket override of Requirement A.

Original_FR: In this case, the user can approve the exchange that is offered by another user by clicking Approve button.
Conflicting_FR: If the exchange approval request is selected, the system shall require the user to manually enter the exchange ID and confirmation code before enabling the "Approve" button.
Resolution:
The conflict here is that Requirement A implies the "Approve" button is directly clickable, while Requirement B states the button should only be enabled after the user manually enters an exchange ID and confirmation code.

1. **Update Requirement A** to explicitly state that the "Approve" button becomes clickable only after the user has successfully entered the required exchange ID and confirmation code, aligning it with the validation gate described in Requirement B.

2. **Revise Requirement B** to distinguish between approval flows: if the user arrives at the approval screen via a direct exchange offer notification (as in Requirement A), the exchange ID and confirmation code may be pre-populated automatically, enabling the "Approve" button without manual entry; manual entry is only required when accessing approval independently.

3. **Introduce a unified workflow requirement** that reconciles both: the system shall display the "Approve" button in a disabled state by default, auto-fill the exchange ID and confirmation code when the user clicks "Approve" from a received exchange offer, and only require manual entry when the approval is initiated without a direct offer context — ensuring both usability (Requirement A) and security validation (Requirement B) are preserved.

Original_FR: In this case, user enters his/her username or email and his/her password and logs in the system.
Conflicting_FR: The user shall log in to the system using a one-time passcode delivered via SMS to their registered mobile number.
Resolution:
1. Implement a multi-factor authentication (MFA) flow where the user first enters their username/email and password (Requirement A), and then receives and enters a one-time passcode via SMS (Requirement B) as a second verification step, combining both requirements into a single secure login process.

2. Define distinct authentication methods based on user role or context: allow standard username/email and password login for general users (Requirement A), while enforcing SMS-based one-time passcode login for users requiring higher security (e.g., admins or sensitive operations), satisfying both requirements in appropriate scenarios (Requirement B).

3. Revise the requirements to offer flexible authentication options, allowing users to choose between password-based login (Requirement A) and SMS one-time passcode login (Requirement B) as alternative methods, while clearly documenting which method is primary and ensuring both are consistently supported across the system.

Original_FR: It is possible to navigate from a building on the campus to a POI on the campus.
Conflicting_FR: When the "Navigate to POI" function is initiated, the system shall require the user to manually input the GPS coordinates for both the starting building and the destination POI before calculating the route.
Resolution:
The conflict here is that Requirement A implies a user-friendly navigation capability (suggesting automatic location detection or simple selection), while Requirement B imposes a cumbersome manual GPS coordinate input process that undermines usability and contradicts the spirit of seamless campus navigation.

1. **Replace manual GPS input with automatic location detection and a selectable POI list:** Revise Requirement B so that the system automatically detects the user's current location (via GPS/Wi-Fi) as the starting point and provides a searchable/selectable list of campus buildings and POIs as the destination, eliminating the need for manual coordinate entry entirely.

2. **Make manual GPS input an optional fallback, not the default:** Amend Requirement B to state that the system shall automatically populate the starting building and destination POI using device location services and a campus POI database, but *optionally* allow users to manually input or override GPS coordinates if automatic detection is unavailable or inaccurate — satisfying both usability and precision needs.

3. **Redefine Requirement B to specify input method as building/POI name selection:** Replace the manual GPS coordinate requirement with a requirement that users select the starting building and destination POI by name or category from a pre-populated campus map interface, ensuring the navigation function described in Requirement A remains practical and accessible while still defining a clear input mechanism for the route calculation.

Original_FR: It is possible to navigate from a POI on the campus to a building on the campus.
Conflicting_FR: If the building destination is selected, the navigation system shall use the building name as the primary input, query the campus database, and calculate the optimal route.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, the potential conflict lies in the fact that Requirement A describes navigation **from a POI to a building** (implying the starting point is a POI), while Requirement B focuses on the **building as a destination** but doesn't account for the case where the origin is a POI rather than a user's current location or another building. This creates ambiguity in how the route calculation is triggered and what inputs are used.

1. **Extend Requirement B to explicitly handle POI origins:** Amend Requirement B to state that when a building destination is selected and the origin is a POI, the system shall use both the POI identifier and the building name as inputs to query the campus database and calculate the optimal route, ensuring POI-to-building navigation is fully supported.

2. **Introduce a unified navigation input model:** Define a common navigation framework that treats both POIs and buildings as valid origin/destination types. Requirement A can reference this model to confirm POI-to-building is a supported use case, while Requirement B is updated to apply its route-calculation logic regardless of whether the origin is a user location, POI, or building.

3. **Clarify scope and precedence between the two requirements:** Explicitly state that Requirement A defines a supported navigation scenario (POI → building), and Requirement B defines the system behavior for any navigation request where the destination is a building. This removes ambiguity by making Requirement B apply as the processing rule whenever a building is the destination, regardless of the origin type specified in Requirement A.

Original_FR: It is possible to navigate from a POI on the campus to a POI on the campus.
Conflicting_FR: When the navigation request is initiated, the system shall require the user to select both a starting building name and a destination building name to proceed.
Resolution:
The conflict here is that Requirement A allows navigation between any POIs (Points of Interest) on campus, while Requirement B restricts navigation inputs specifically to **building names**, excluding other POIs such as parking lots, outdoor spaces, landmarks, or entrances.

1. **Expand Requirement B to include all POI types:** Modify Requirement B so that the system requires the user to select both a starting **POI** and a destination **POI** (which may include buildings, landmarks, parking areas, etc.), rather than restricting selection to building names only. This aligns B with the broader scope defined in A.

2. **Clarify Requirement A to be building-scoped:** If the intent is truly limited to buildings, revise Requirement A to explicitly state that navigation is between **building POIs** on campus, making both requirements consistent in scope and eliminating the ambiguity around non-building POIs.

3. **Introduce a tiered selection model:** Retain Requirement B's building-name selection as the primary/default navigation mode, but amend it to also allow selection of **other POI categories** (e.g., outdoor landmarks, parking lots) as valid start/destination options. This preserves the structured selection flow of B while fully satisfying the broader navigation capability described in A.

Original_FR: It is possible to search a person by department.
Conflicting_FR: "The system shall only accept a personnel ID number as the sole input for any search function."
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement B to allow multiple search criteria by changing it to: "The system shall accept a personnel ID number, department, or other approved attributes as valid inputs for search functions," making department-based search explicitly permitted.

2. Introduce a role-based search policy where Requirement B applies only to specific restricted search contexts (e.g., security-sensitive lookups), while Requirement A governs general HR or directory search functions, clearly scoping each requirement to its intended use case.

3. Redesign the search function to internally map department queries to personnel ID numbers, so that users can search by department (satisfying Requirement A) while the system technically processes results using personnel ID numbers (satisfying Requirement B's intent of standardized identification).

Original_FR: It is possible to search a person by name.
Conflicting_FR: The search function shall accept only a unique personnel identification number as the input criteria.
Resolution:
1. Extend the search function to support both input types by allowing users to search either by name or by unique personnel identification number, making both fields valid search criteria while keeping the ID-based search as the primary/default option.

2. Clarify the scope of each requirement by splitting them into two distinct search modes: a "quick search" accepting the unique personnel identification number for precise lookups, and an "advanced search" accepting a name for broader queries, ensuring both needs are met without contradiction.

3. Revise Requirement B to redefine the unique personnel identification number as the mandatory output/result identifier rather than the sole input criteria, allowing name-based searches (Requirement A) to be performed while ensuring results are always resolved and displayed using the unique ID to avoid ambiguity.

Original_FR: It is possible to search for a certain building on the campus of the TU/e by address.
Conflicting_FR: "The system shall search for a building by its unique internal building code, which is generated upon its addition to the campus database."
Resolution:
1. Combine both search methods into a unified search interface that accepts either an address or an internal building code as input, allowing users to find buildings using whichever identifier they have available.

2. Clarify the intended use cases and user roles for each requirement: if the address-based search is intended for end users (students, visitors) and the building code search is intended for system administrators or internal processes, define separate search functionalities tailored to each audience.

3. Introduce a flexible search mechanism that treats both the address and the internal building code as valid, non-exclusive search attributes, and update the requirements to explicitly state that a building can be retrieved by either identifier, resolving the implicit assumption that only one search method should exist.

Original_FR: Mobile application - Search by destination DESC: A user should be able to input a maximum and a minimum distance, according to his/her position. By default the minimum distance is set to 0 km and the maximum to 10 km. The user should be able to input a higher or lower maximum distance and a higher minimum distance than set by default. The result is displayed in a map view by default.
Conflicting_FR: "Mobile application - Search by destination DESC: A user should be able to input a specific destination name or address. The system shall ignore any distance parameters and search exclusively for locations matching the provided destination text. The result is displayed in a list view by default."
Resolution:
Looking at the conflict between these two requirements, the main issues are: (1) conflicting default display views (map vs. list), and (2) the interaction between distance parameters and destination text search (Requirement B ignores distance parameters entirely, while Requirement A centers on them).

1. **Separate the two search modes explicitly**: Define "Search by Distance" and "Search by Destination" as two distinct, mutually exclusive search modes within the UI. Each mode has its own parameters — distance-based search uses min/max distance inputs and defaults to map view, while destination text search uses a name/address input and defaults to list view. This eliminates ambiguity about which parameters apply when.

2. **Establish a unified default display view**: Align both requirements on a single default display view (e.g., map view) to remove the contradiction, while allowing users to toggle between map and list views in both search modes. This preserves flexibility without conflicting defaults across requirements.

3. **Define parameter priority rules for combined search**: If both search modes must coexist in a single interface, specify clear precedence rules — e.g., when a destination name/address is entered, distance parameters are disabled/greyed out in the UI, and the default view switches to list. When only distance parameters are used, the map view is the default. This makes the behavior deterministic and resolves the conflict through explicit conditional logic.

Original_FR: Once the icon is selected two open text field will be available to the user to search for the product name - by product ID/SKU or by keyword.
Conflicting_FR: Once the icon is selected, the THEMAS system shall automatically populate the search fields using the user's last search criteria and execute the search without requiring manual input.
Resolution:
1. **Combine both behaviors sequentially**: When the icon is selected, the system shall automatically populate the search fields with the user's last search criteria (if available) and execute the search, while still allowing the user to manually modify the pre-filled text fields and re-execute a new search if desired. This satisfies both auto-population and manual input capabilities.

2. **Introduce a user preference setting**: Add a configurable option allowing users to choose between two modes — "Auto-Search Mode" (system pre-fills fields with last search criteria and executes automatically) or "Manual Search Mode" (empty text fields are presented for fresh input). This resolves the conflict by making both behaviors valid based on user preference.

3. **Clarify scope and priority through stakeholder alignment**: Treat Requirement A as the baseline UI specification (two open text fields are always visible) and Requirement B as an enhancement layer (fields are pre-populated with last criteria and auto-executed). Explicitly update the requirements to state that open text fields are pre-filled with the last search criteria upon icon selection, the search executes automatically, but the fields remain editable — eliminating ambiguity about whether fields should be empty or pre-populated.

Original_FR: Problems entered on the encounter form by clerical personnel shall be left as UNVERIFIED.
Conflicting_FR: Problems entered on the encounter form by clerical personnel shall be left as UNSIGNED.
Resolution:
1. Merge the two requirements into a single requirement that assigns both statuses simultaneously, specifying that problems entered by clerical personnel shall be marked as both **UNVERIFIED and UNSIGNED**, clearly defining each term's distinct meaning (e.g., UNVERIFIED = pending clinical validation, UNSIGNED = lacking authorized signature).

2. Clarify and separate the concerns by defining a status workflow: problems entered by clerical personnel shall initially be set to **UNSIGNED**, and upon clinical review without approval, transition to **UNVERIFIED**, documenting the distinction and sequence between the two statuses in a shared glossary or data dictionary.

3. Convene a stakeholder review with both clinical and administrative teams to determine which single status (**UNVERIFIED** or **UNSIGNED**) is the authoritative designation for clerical entries, eliminate the conflicting requirement, and update all related documentation to reflect the agreed-upon standard consistently.

Original_FR: Problems entered on the encounter form by clerical personnel shall be left as UNVERIFIED.
Conflicting_FR: Problems entered on the encounter form by clerical personnel shall be left as UNSIGNED.
Resolution:
1. **Merge the statuses by applying both labels simultaneously**: Define a combined status "UNVERIFIED/UNSIGNED" that satisfies both requirements, ensuring problems entered by clerical personnel are flagged as neither verified nor signed until a qualified clinician reviews and countersigns them.

2. **Clarify and differentiate the two statuses as sequential workflow states**: Define "UNVERIFIED" as the initial data-entry status (indicating the problem has not been clinically validated) and "UNSIGNED" as a separate status (indicating it lacks a clinician's signature/authorization), then specify that clerical entries must carry both statuses simultaneously or transition through them in a defined order.

3. **Escalate to stakeholders for a single authoritative status definition**: Convene the relevant clinical, administrative, and technical stakeholders to agree on one canonical status term (either UNVERIFIED or UNSIGNED, or a newly defined term) that captures the intended meaning for clerical entries, then update both requirements to reference that single, consistently defined status across all documentation.

Original_FR: Product formulas shall allow use of semi-products which have own formulas.
Conflicting_FR: Product formulas shall only use raw materials as input and shall not allow the use of semi-products.
Resolution:
1. Clarify scope by introducing a configuration flag or system parameter that allows users to toggle between "flat formulas" (raw materials only) and "multi-level formulas" (allowing semi-products), so both use cases are supported without contradiction.

2. Redefine the requirements to apply to different product types or contexts: Requirement B applies to simple/standard products where only raw materials are permitted, while Requirement A applies to complex/composite products where semi-products with their own sub-formulas are explicitly allowed, clearly documenting which rule governs which product category.

3. Replace both conflicting requirements with a single unified requirement that explicitly states the intended behavior, for example: "Product formulas shall support both raw materials and semi-products as inputs; however, the use of semi-products must be explicitly enabled per formula and each semi-product must have a fully defined sub-formula," thereby eliminating the contradiction while preserving the intent of both stakeholders.

Original_FR: Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members shall be able to add a listing of students to a cohort.
Conflicting_FR: The THEMAS system shall only accept the addition of students to a cohort via an uploaded .CSV file containing the complete student roster.
Resolution:
1. Expand Requirement B to support multiple input methods by allowing both manual entry/listing and CSV file upload, so that Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members can add students either individually or in bulk via CSV, giving flexibility while retaining the CSV option.

2. Clarify the scope of each requirement by restricting CSV-only uploads to bulk/initial cohort creation (Requirement B) while permitting manual addition of individual students by authorized roles (Requirement A), effectively making the two requirements complementary rather than conflicting.

3. Revise Requirement B to remove the "only" constraint and instead specify that CSV upload is the *preferred or primary* method for adding a complete student roster, while Requirement A governs the general permission model — ensuring authorized roles retain the ability to add students through any system-supported method.

Original_FR: Required information for registration Given the restaurant owner wants to create an account And the restaurant owner does not have an account When the restaurant owner registers on the web-portal by providing user-name And password And address And e-mail address And phone number Then the restaurant owner should be able to apply for verification
Conflicting_FR: "Required information for registration Given the restaurant owner wants to create an account And the restaurant owner does not have an account When the restaurant owner registers on the web-portal by providing user-name And password And business license number Then the restaurant owner should be able to apply for verification."
Resolution:
1. Merge both requirements into a single unified registration requirement that includes all fields: username, password, address, e-mail address, phone number, and business license number, ensuring all necessary personal and business verification information is collected in one registration flow.

2. Separate the fields into mandatory and optional categories — treat username, password, and business license number as mandatory fields (core to identity and verification), while making address, e-mail address, and phone number required but clearly defined as contact/communication details, resolving ambiguity about what is strictly needed for verification.

3. Conduct a stakeholder review with both business and technical teams to determine the minimum viable set of registration fields required for verification purposes, then update both requirements to reflect a single agreed-upon definition, eliminating the duplication and contradiction between the two versions.

Original_FR: System will allow customers to pay for a streaming movie with a pre-paid card.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall accept payment only via a credit or debit card entered at the time of the streaming transaction.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement B to explicitly include pre-paid cards as an accepted payment method alongside credit and debit cards, since pre-paid cards function similarly and can be processed through the same payment infrastructure (e.g., "The system shall accept payment via credit, debit, or pre-paid card entered at the time of the streaming transaction").

2. Clarify the definition of "pre-paid card" in Requirement A to specify whether it refers to a pre-paid Visa/Mastercard-style card (which behaves like a debit card and could satisfy Requirement B) or a proprietary gift/voucher card, and update both requirements accordingly to reflect the agreed-upon scope.

3. If both payment types are intentionally supported, consolidate the two requirements into a single, unified payment requirement that lists all accepted payment methods (credit card, debit card, pre-paid card) and the condition that payment must be entered at the time of the transaction, eliminating the contradiction and serving as a single source of truth.

Original_FR: The administrator can add vertices of a path which is used in the route planner.
Conflicting_FR: If the administrator is adding a path vertex, the system shall require all vertex coordinates to be manually entered via a formatted text file upload.
Resolution:
The conflict here is that Requirement A implies a general ability to add path vertices (leaving the method open/flexible), while Requirement B restricts the input method exclusively to a formatted text file upload, eliminating any other potential input methods (e.g., manual UI entry, map clicking, coordinate pickers).

1. **Expand Requirement B to allow multiple input methods:** Revise Requirement B to support both manual coordinate entry through the UI *and* bulk upload via a formatted text file, giving administrators flexibility (e.g., "The system shall allow vertex coordinates to be entered individually via a form interface *or* uploaded in bulk via a formatted text file").

2. **Clarify scope and context of each requirement:** If the text file upload is only intended for bulk/batch operations, explicitly scope Requirement B to that scenario (e.g., "When adding multiple path vertices in bulk, the system shall support a formatted text file upload"), while Requirement A covers the general single-vertex addition use case, eliminating the overlap and contradiction.

3. **Designate one requirement as the authoritative input method and update accordingly:** Conduct a stakeholder review to decide whether file upload is mandatory or optional. If manual UI entry is also needed, remove the exclusivity implied by "shall require" in Requirement B and replace it with "shall support," making file upload one of several accepted methods rather than the only one.

Original_FR: The audit report shall include the total number of recycled parts used in the estimate.
Conflicting_FR: The THEMAS system shall generate the audit report using only the parts data from the primary supplier database.
Resolution:
1. Expand Requirement B to allow the THEMAS system to query additional data sources (e.g., a recycled parts registry or secondary supplier database) alongside the primary supplier database, ensuring the audit report can accurately include recycled parts totals without restricting the data scope.

2. Amend Requirement A to specify that the total number of recycled parts shall be included in the audit report only when such data is available and tagged within the primary supplier database, and update the primary supplier database schema to include a "recycled" flag or attribute for all parts records.

3. Introduce a data integration requirement that mandates recycled parts information be consolidated into the primary supplier database prior to audit report generation, reconciling both requirements by ensuring the single authoritative data source contains all necessary recycled parts data to satisfy Requirement A while preserving the constraint in Requirement B.

Original_FR: The client will send a GET request to the Web API with the question as a URL parameter The client will specify the header Content-Type: application/json in their requests as convention. A valid API query is a single URL parameter containing one sentence that is a question in standard English.
Conflicting_FR: The client will send a POST request to the Web API with the question in the request body. The client must specify the header Content-Type: application/json and the request body shall be a JSON object containing a single key "question" with the value being the question in standard English. A valid API query is a JSON payload and URL parameters are not accepted.
Resolution:
1. Standardize on POST with a JSON request body (adopting Requirement B), as it better aligns with REST best practices for sending structured data, avoids exposing the question in the URL, and enforces a consistent JSON contract; update all documentation and client implementations to remove the GET/URL-parameter approach entirely.

2. If backward compatibility is needed, support both methods by implementing a versioned API (e.g., `/v1/` for GET with URL parameter, `/v2/` for POST with JSON body), clearly documenting each version's expected format and deprecation timeline so clients can migrate to the POST endpoint.

3. Convene a stakeholder review to select one canonical HTTP method and input format, then produce a single, authoritative API specification (e.g., an OpenAPI/Swagger document) that all teams must follow, eliminating the ambiguity at the source and preventing future divergence between client and server expectations.

Original_FR: The Disputes System will provide the user the ability to create or initiate a ticket retrieval request. As part of ticket retrieval creation process the system must prompt the user to enter all the required information to create the ticket retrieval request. The ticket retrieval request is a document that is sent to merchant inquiring the validity of a transaction.
Conflicting_FR: If the user selects the quick retrieval option, the Disputes System shall automatically generate a ticket retrieval request using default values for all required fields and submit it to the merchant without prompting the user for any information.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a dual-mode design where Requirement A governs the standard retrieval flow (prompting the user for all required fields) and Requirement B governs an explicitly separate "Quick Retrieval" flow that bypasses prompts using predefined defaults, clearly documenting both paths as distinct use cases in the specification.

2. Amend Requirement A to include an exception clause stating that the system must prompt the user for all required information *unless* the user selects the quick retrieval option, thereby making Requirement B a recognized and explicitly permitted override of the standard prompting behavior.

3. Define and standardize the "default values" referenced in Requirement B (e.g., pre-configured merchant profiles, transaction data auto-populated from the dispute record) and require the system to display a confirmation summary screen before submission, giving the user a final opportunity to review or cancel — satisfying the spirit of user awareness in Requirement A while preserving the streamlined flow of Requirement B.

Original_FR: The feature of the application shall provide the optimized route for the given addresses
Conflicting_FR: The route optimization feature shall require the user to select a specific vehicle type and input a maximum acceptable toll cost before calculating the route.
Resolution:
The conflict here is that Requirement A implies a straightforward, automatic route optimization based on addresses alone, while Requirement B introduces mandatory preconditions (vehicle type selection and maximum toll cost input) that add complexity and potential friction to the process.

1. Make vehicle type and toll cost inputs **optional with smart defaults**: Modify Requirement B so that vehicle type defaults to a standard car and maximum toll cost defaults to unlimited, allowing the system to calculate an optimized route immediately (satisfying Requirement A) while still giving users the option to refine results via Requirement B's parameters.

2. **Split into two modes**: Define a "Quick Route" mode that satisfies Requirement A by calculating an optimized route from addresses alone, and an "Advanced Route" mode that satisfies Requirement B by requiring vehicle type and toll cost inputs — clearly documenting both modes in the requirements to eliminate ambiguity.

3. **Revise Requirement B to use a progressive disclosure approach**: Rewrite Requirement B so that the system first calculates and displays the optimized route (fulfilling Requirement A), then prompts the user to optionally refine the route by specifying vehicle type and maximum toll cost, re-optimizing only if the user provides those inputs — ensuring base functionality is never blocked.

Original_FR: The from-location can be searched by the address of a building.
Conflicting_FR: The from-location shall be selected from a pre-defined list of available depots.
Resolution:
1. Allow users to search by building address, but restrict results to only display matching entries from the pre-defined list of available depots, combining free-text search convenience with the constraint of valid depot selection.

2. Clarify the scope of each requirement by designating the pre-defined depot list as the authoritative source for the from-location, while providing address-based search purely as a lookup/filter mechanism to help users quickly find and select the correct depot from that list.

3. Introduce a two-tier selection model: users may search by building address to identify a nearby or relevant location, but the system must then map and confirm the result to a valid depot from the pre-defined list before finalizing the from-location, ensuring compliance with both requirements.

Original_FR: The from-location can be searched by the name of a building.
Conflicting_FR: The from-location shall be determined exclusively by the GPS coordinates provided by the user's device.
Resolution:
1. Allow GPS coordinates as the default method for determining the from-location, but provide a manual override option that lets users search by building name, with the searched location replacing the GPS-derived coordinates.

2. Redefine the from-location input to support multiple input methods (GPS coordinates and building name search), where building name search resolves to a set of coordinates, ensuring GPS coordinates remain the underlying representation while accommodating both requirements.

3. Clarify the intended use context by splitting the requirements: use GPS coordinates as the primary/automatic from-location determination, and allow building name search only as a fallback when GPS is unavailable or inaccurate, removing the "exclusively" constraint from Requirement B to accommodate both methods.

Original_FR: The from-location can be searched by the name of a POI.
Conflicting_FR: "The from-location can be searched by the name of a POI or by entering its precise latitude and longitude coordinates."
Resolution:
1. **Merge into a single, comprehensive requirement:** Replace both requirements with a unified statement: "The from-location can be searched by the name of a POI or by entering its precise latitude and longitude coordinates," effectively adopting Requirement B as the authoritative version since it is a superset of Requirement A.

2. **Clarify scope and prioritize:** If the two requirements originate from different stakeholders or development phases, explicitly mark Requirement A as superseded by Requirement B, and update the requirements traceability matrix to reflect that Requirement B fully covers and extends Requirement A, avoiding duplication.

3. **Decompose into sub-requirements:** Rewrite as a parent requirement with two distinct sub-requirements — (a) "The from-location can be searched by the name of a POI" and (b) "The from-location can be searched by entering precise latitude and longitude coordinates" — ensuring both search methods are independently testable and traceable while eliminating the overlap between the original two requirements.

Original_FR: The from-location is selected from the search result described in UCAR211 and UCAR212.
Conflicting_FR: The from-location is entered manually by the user in a free-text field and must be validated against a pre-approved master location list before the search is initiated.
Resolution:
1. **Adopt a hybrid input model**: Allow the user to either select a from-location from the search results (as per UCAR211/UCAR212) or manually enter it in a free-text field, but apply the master location list validation in both cases before initiating the search. This satisfies both the structured selection and manual entry needs while enforcing data integrity.

2. **Prioritize search-based selection with a manual fallback**: Define the primary interaction as selecting from the search results (UCAR211/UCAR212), but permit manual free-text entry only when no matching result is found. In the fallback case, validate the manually entered value against the pre-approved master location list before proceeding, clearly documenting this as an exception flow.

3. **Clarify scope and ownership with stakeholders**: If the two requirements originate from different use cases or user roles, engage stakeholders to determine which interaction model takes precedence or whether both are needed. Revise the requirements to explicitly state the conditions under which each input method applies (e.g., online vs. offline mode, standard user vs. power user), eliminating the ambiguity and potential overlap.

Original_FR: The IQA subsystem shall take into account current inventory status and adjust inventory of substitutionary ingredients accordingly.
Conflicting_FR: The IQA subsystem shall take into account the forecasted demand for all products and adjust inventory of substitutionary ingredients accordingly.
Resolution:
The core conflict here is that Requirement A focuses on **current (real-time) inventory status** as the trigger for adjusting substitutionary ingredient inventory, while Requirement B focuses on **forecasted demand** as the trigger — these two signals can lead to contradictory adjustment decisions if not reconciled.

1. **Merge into a unified requirement** that explicitly combines both inputs: *"The IQA subsystem shall take into account both the current inventory status and the forecasted demand for all products, and apply a defined priority rule (e.g., forecasted demand overrides current status, or a weighted blending algorithm) to adjust inventory of substitutionary ingredients accordingly."* This eliminates ambiguity about which signal drives decisions.

2. **Separate the two requirements into distinct, sequenced processing steps** within the IQA workflow: first, evaluate forecasted demand to establish a target inventory level for substitutionary ingredients, then apply current inventory status as a real-time correction factor to adjust from that target. This makes both inputs complementary rather than conflicting.

3. **Define explicit conflict-resolution thresholds and business rules** that specify when each requirement takes precedence — for example, if current inventory falls below a critical safety stock level, current inventory status takes priority; otherwise, forecasted demand drives adjustments. Document these rules as acceptance criteria to ensure both requirements are testable and consistently implemented.

Original_FR: The leads washing functionality shall receive a period list of available reps from the enterprise system.
Conflicting_FR: "The leads washing functionality shall receive a list of available reps from the local sales management system."
Resolution:
1. **Consolidate into a single source of truth**: Define one authoritative system (either the enterprise system or the local sales management system) as the primary source for available reps, and update both requirements to reference that single source, eliminating the ambiguity.

2. **Introduce a data aggregation/integration layer**: Revise the requirements so that the leads washing functionality receives the list of available reps from a unified integration service or middleware that synchronizes and merges rep data from both the enterprise system and the local sales management system, ensuring consistency.

3. **Clarify scope and context for each requirement**: Investigate whether the two requirements address different use cases or deployment contexts (e.g., enterprise-wide vs. local/regional operations). If so, clearly differentiate the scenarios in which each source applies and rewrite the requirements with explicit conditions (e.g., "when operating in enterprise mode, use the enterprise system; when operating in local mode, use the local sales management system").

Original_FR: The leads washing functionality will return the lead data supplied to the vendor along with the reason of rejection.
Conflicting_FR: The leads washing functionality shall process the lead data without requiring the original lead details, using only a unique transaction identifier provided by the vendor.
Resolution:
1. **Adopt a hybrid approach**: Redesign the leads washing functionality to accept either the full lead data or a unique transaction identifier. When only a transaction identifier is provided, the system shall internally retrieve the associated lead data, process it, and return the lead details along with the rejection reason — satisfying both the vendor's flexibility needs and the requirement to return lead data with rejection reasons.

2. **Clarify and prioritize the vendor interaction model**: Conduct a stakeholder review to determine the intended vendor workflow — whether vendors submit full lead data or only a transaction identifier. Based on the outcome, retain one requirement as the primary design constraint and revise or retire the other, ensuring the final requirement clearly specifies both the input method and the expected output (including rejection reasons).

3. **Separate input and output concerns**: Redefine Requirement B to address only the *input* mechanism (i.e., vendors may submit a transaction identifier instead of raw lead data), while preserving Requirement A's *output* specification (i.e., the system must always return the original lead data and rejection reason). The system would resolve the full lead details from the transaction identifier internally before processing, decoupling how data is submitted from how results are reported.

Original_FR: The leads washing functionality will use the parameters supplied by corporate and schools to score part of the lead.
Conflicting_FR: If the leads washing functionality is selected, the system shall use the parameters supplied by the regional sales teams and partners to score the entire lead.
Resolution:
1. **Unify the scoring scope and parameter sources**: Rewrite the requirements into a single requirement that clearly defines which stakeholders (corporate, schools, regional sales teams, and/or partners) provide scoring parameters, and explicitly state whether the lead is scored in full or in part. For example: "When leads washing is selected, the system shall use parameters supplied by corporate, schools, regional sales teams, and partners to score the entire lead, with each stakeholder group contributing parameters for their designated scoring segments."

2. **Introduce a tiered/modular scoring model**: Define a composite scoring approach where corporate and schools supply parameters that score a defined subset (partial) of the lead, while regional sales teams and partners supply parameters that cover the remaining portions — together constituting the full lead score. This reconciles "part of the lead" vs. "entire lead" by making both true within a layered architecture.

3. **Clarify activation conditions and precedence rules**: Determine whether Requirement A applies when leads washing is *not* explicitly selected (default/background scoring) and Requirement B applies only when it *is* explicitly selected, or establish a clear precedence rule. Document the distinction so that both requirements can coexist with non-overlapping trigger conditions and stakeholder responsibilities, eliminating the ambiguity around scope and parameter ownership.

Original_FR: The maximum number of concurrently running units shall be read from the initialization file and stored in the THEMAS system.
Conflicting_FR: The maximum number of concurrently running units must be read from the initialization file and replicated in the THEMAS system.
Resolution:
The core conflict here lies in the terminology: Requirement A uses **"stored"** while Requirement B uses **"replicated"**, implying different data management strategies (single storage vs. distributed/redundant copies).

1. **Unify the terminology by clarifying the intended storage behavior**: Convene a stakeholder review to determine whether the value should be stored in a single location ("stored") or maintained across multiple nodes/instances ("replicated"). Update both requirements to use a single, agreed-upon term that accurately reflects the system's architectural need.

2. **Merge the two requirements into one authoritative requirement**: Replace both with a single requirement that explicitly states the reading mechanism and the storage strategy, for example: *"The maximum number of concurrently running units shall be read from the initialization file and [stored/replicated] in the THEMAS system in accordance with the system's data consistency architecture."* This eliminates duplication and removes ambiguity.

3. **Decompose into separate, complementary requirements**: If both behaviors are intentionally needed, split the concern into two distinct requirements — one specifying the *reading* action (shared by both) and one specifying the *storage strategy* (stored vs. replicated), with clear traceability links. This preserves both intents without contradiction and allows each behavior to be independently verified and tested.

Original_FR: The OPENCOSS platform must provide users with the ability to modify the intermediate nodes in the tree-view for display of the evidence items of an assurance project only once.
Conflicting_FR: The OPENCOSS platform shall provide users with the ability to modify the intermediate nodes in the tree-view for display of the evidence items of an assurance project.
Resolution:
The core conflict here is that Requirement A restricts modification of intermediate nodes to **only once**, while Requirement B places **no such restriction**, allowing unlimited modifications.

1. **Adopt Requirement B with a configurable restriction policy:** Remove the "only once" constraint from Requirement A and use Requirement B as the baseline, but introduce an optional administrative setting that allows project owners to lock nodes after a single modification if desired, satisfying both the unrestricted and restricted use cases.

2. **Clarify and merge intent into a single requirement:** Engage stakeholders to determine whether the "only once" restriction in Requirement A was intentional (e.g., for audit/traceability reasons) or an error. If intentional, rewrite a single unified requirement that explicitly states the business rationale (e.g., "users may modify intermediate nodes, with changes logged and limited to one modification per node to ensure traceability"). If unintentional, simply retire Requirement A in favor of Requirement B.

3. **Introduce a role-based modification rule:** Replace both requirements with a single requirement that differentiates by user role — for example, standard users may modify intermediate nodes only once (preserving the spirit of Requirement A), while administrators or project managers may modify them multiple times (preserving the flexibility of Requirement B), thereby reconciling the conflict through access control.

Original_FR: The OPENCOSS platform shall be able to automatically associate an artefact in an external tool to an evidence item of an assurance project imported from the external tool.
Conflicting_FR: The OPENCOSS platform must be able to automatically indicate the tool that stores the artefact associated to an evidence item of an assurance project imported from external tool.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, they are actually complementary rather than strictly conflicting — Requirement A focuses on **associating** an artefact to an evidence item, while Requirement B focuses on **indicating the tool** that stores that artefact. However, there is a potential overlap/ambiguity in scope and responsibility that could cause implementation conflicts. Here are resolution suggestions:

1. **Merge into a single unified requirement** that combines both capabilities: "The OPENCOSS platform shall automatically associate an artefact in an external tool to an evidence item of an assurance project imported from that tool, and shall indicate which external tool stores the associated artefact." This eliminates redundancy and clarifies that both association and tool identification are part of the same operation.

2. **Clarify the sequencing and dependency** by explicitly stating that Requirement B is a sub-function of Requirement A — i.e., the automatic association (Req A) must include, as a mandatory attribute, the identification of the source tool (Req B). This ensures the tool indication is treated as a required metadata element of the association process rather than a separate feature.

3. **Define clear data model boundaries** by specifying that the evidence item record must contain two distinct but linked fields: one for the artefact association (satisfying Req A) and one for the tool identifier (satisfying Req B). This separates the concerns at the design level while ensuring both requirements are fully and independently traceable and verifiable.

Original_FR: The OPENCOSS platform shall provide users with the choice to drop evidence traceability links of an assurance project.
Conflicting_FR: The OPENCOSS platform shall provide users with the ability to drop evidence traceability links of an assurance project.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, the apparent "conflict" is subtle — Requirement A uses "choice" while Requirement B uses "ability," which may imply slightly different nuances (user discretion/optionality vs. functional capability). Here are resolution suggestions:

1. **Merge into a single unified requirement** that captures both the functional capability and the user discretion aspect, e.g.: *"The OPENCOSS platform shall provide users with the ability to optionally drop evidence traceability links of an assurance project,"* eliminating redundancy while preserving both intents.

2. **Clarify and differentiate the intent of each requirement**: If "choice" implies a deliberate confirmation step (e.g., a prompt asking the user to confirm before dropping links) and "ability" implies the underlying functional feature, rewrite them as distinct, complementary requirements — one specifying the functional capability and the other specifying the user confirmation/consent mechanism.

3. **Adopt consistent terminology across the requirements specification**: Select one preferred term ("ability" for functional capabilities, "choice" for user-facing options) and apply it consistently throughout the document, then consolidate these two requirements into one authoritative statement to avoid duplication and potential misinterpretation during implementation.

Original_FR: The product shall display a map of the company building showing conference room locations.
Conflicting_FR: When the user requests the map of the company building, the system shall require the user to input a specific conference room number to proceed.
Resolution:
The conflict here is that Requirement A implies displaying a full map showing all conference room locations, while Requirement B restricts access by requiring a specific room number input before proceeding — effectively preventing the full map from being shown upfront.

1. **Show the full map by default, with optional filtering:** Display the complete building map with all conference room locations visible (satisfying Req A), and provide an optional search/filter field where users can enter a specific room number to highlight or zoom into that room (satisfying Req B's intent without blocking initial map access).

2. **Clarify and separate the two use cases:** Rewrite the requirements to distinguish between two distinct interactions — (a) a general map view that displays all conference room locations, and (b) a detailed room lookup flow that requires a room number input for additional details (e.g., availability, capacity). This eliminates the conflict by assigning each requirement to a different feature.

3. **Remove the mandatory gate in Requirement B:** Revise Requirement B so that entering a room number is optional rather than required to proceed. The system should display the full map immediately, and only prompt for a room number if the user wishes to retrieve specific room details, making the input a convenience feature rather than a prerequisite.

Original_FR: The product shall display room equipment according to search parameters.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall display room equipment from a pre-defined, static list and ignore all user-provided search parameters.
Resolution:
1. Retain Requirement A and remove Requirement B, as dynamic search-based filtering better serves user needs; replace the static list concept with a searchable database that responds to user-provided parameters.

2. Clarify the intent behind Requirement B by determining whether the static list was meant as a fallback/default view (e.g., when no search parameters are provided), and revise both requirements so that the static list is shown initially but filtered dynamically when the user supplies search parameters.

3. Engage stakeholders to confirm the authoritative business need, then rewrite a single, unified requirement such as: "The system shall display room equipment from a defined inventory, filtered and sorted according to user-provided search parameters; if no parameters are provided, all available equipment shall be displayed by default."

Original_FR: The supervisor’s interface to the THEMAS system shall provide a mechanism to select between an operational history report or a statistical summary report.
Conflicting_FR: The supervisor’s interface to the THEMAS system shall provide a mechanism to select between an operational history report or a dynamic summary report.
Resolution:
1. Clarify with stakeholders whether "statistical summary report" and "dynamic summary report" refer to the same report type under different names, and if so, standardize on a single agreed-upon term across all requirements documentation.

2. If both report types are genuinely distinct, update the requirements to explicitly include all three report options (operational history, statistical summary, and dynamic summary), clearly defining the purpose and content of each to eliminate ambiguity.

3. Conduct a review with the product owner and end users to determine which report type (statistical or dynamic summary) better meets operational needs, then update both requirements to consistently reference the chosen report type, retiring the outdated term.

Original_FR: The supervisor’s interface to the THEMAS system shall provide a mechanism to select between an operational history report or a statistical summary report.
Conflicting_FR: The supervisor’s interface shall provide a mechanism to select between an operational history report or a dynamic summary report.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended report type by engaging stakeholders to determine whether the second report should be called "statistical summary report" or "dynamic summary report," and unify the terminology across both requirements into a single, agreed-upon requirement.

2. If both report types are genuinely needed, merge the two requirements into one that explicitly offers three options: an operational history report, a statistical summary report, and a dynamic summary report, clearly defining the distinction between the latter two.

3. Define and document the difference between "statistical summary report" and "dynamic summary report" in a glossary or requirements specification; if they refer to the same artifact, eliminate the duplicate requirement and standardize on one term, or if they are distinct, update both requirements to reflect their unique purposes unambiguously.

Original_FR: The system shall allow clinicians to create patient lists for who can sign or cosign for each other.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall only allow clinicians to create patient lists for who can sign or cosign for each other.
Resolution:
The conflict here is subtle but important: Requirement A uses "allow" (permissive, implying others may also have this capability), while Requirement B uses "only allow" (restrictive, implying exclusive access limited to clinicians). The conflict centers on **who else**, if anyone, can create these patient lists.

1. **Clarify the authorized user scope explicitly:** Revise the requirement to clearly enumerate all roles permitted to create patient lists (e.g., clinicians, administrators, supervisors). If only clinicians are intended, adopt Requirement B's restrictive wording. If other roles should also have access, retain Requirement A's permissive framing and list those roles explicitly.

2. **Introduce role-based access control (RBAC) language:** Merge both requirements into a single, unambiguous statement such as: *"The system shall restrict the creation of patient sign/cosign lists exclusively to authorized clinicians, unless administrative override permissions are explicitly granted to designated roles."* This resolves the tension by defining the default restriction while accommodating legitimate exceptions.

3. **Engage stakeholders to resolve intent:** Conduct a requirements review session with clinical workflow owners and security/compliance stakeholders to determine whether the "only" restriction is a hard security/compliance constraint (e.g., regulatory requirement) or an oversight. Document the decision as a rationale note attached to the final requirement to prevent future ambiguity.

Original_FR: The system shall allow concurrent membership in more than one User Class.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall allow membership in only one User Class.
Resolution:
1. Clarify scope by introducing a distinction between "primary" and "secondary" user classes, allowing a single mandatory primary class (satisfying Requirement B's intent of a clear default role) while permitting optional additional class memberships (satisfying Requirement A's flexibility).

2. Engage stakeholders to determine the actual business need and replace both requirements with a single, unambiguous requirement that explicitly states whether single or multiple class membership is required, along with any conditions or exceptions (e.g., "The system shall allow a user to belong to exactly one User Class at a time, but an administrator may reassign the user to a different class as needed").

3. Resolve the conflict by making class membership context-dependent: rewrite the requirements so that certain user types or modules enforce single-class membership while others permit multiple memberships, clearly documenting the rules and conditions under which each policy applies to eliminate the contradiction.

Original_FR: The system shall allow document types to inherit authorizations from a higher level when using classes to assign roles.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall allow document types to inherit authorizations from other levels when using classes to assign roles.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, the conflict centers on the **scope of inheritance**: Requirement A restricts inheritance to a "higher level" (upward/parent direction only), while Requirement B allows inheritance from "other levels" (broader, potentially including peer or lower levels).

1. **Clarify and unify the inheritance direction** by replacing both requirements with a single, precise requirement that explicitly defines which levels are permitted (e.g., "The system shall allow document types to inherit authorizations from parent/ancestor levels only when using classes to assign roles"), eliminating ambiguity about whether peer or child-level inheritance is intended.

2. **Decompose into separate, scoped requirements** by keeping Requirement A as the baseline (mandatory upward inheritance) and rewriting Requirement B as an optional or configurable extension (e.g., "The system shall optionally allow document types to inherit authorizations from peer or lower levels when explicitly configured"), making the distinction between required and optional behavior clear.

3. **Engage stakeholders to define the intended inheritance model** by conducting a review session to determine whether the broader "other levels" in Requirement B was intentional (e.g., supporting bidirectional or lateral inheritance) or a drafting error meant to align with Requirement A, then updating both requirements to reflect a single agreed-upon inheritance hierarchy with documented rationale.

Original_FR: The system shall allow infinite hierarchies of subclasses to be created in the User Hierarchy Class structure.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall allow 100 hierarchies of subclasses to be created in the User Hierarchy Class structure.
Resolution:
1. Adopt Requirement B as the definitive constraint, replacing "infinite" with a clearly defined limit of 100 hierarchies, and update Requirement A accordingly to eliminate the ambiguity introduced by "infinite."

2. Clarify the intent behind both requirements with stakeholders: if "infinite" was aspirational and 100 is the practical system limit, rewrite the single unified requirement to state "the system shall support up to 100 levels of subclass hierarchies, with the architecture designed to allow this limit to be increased in future releases."

3. Separate the concerns by defining a configurable limit: specify that the system shall enforce a maximum hierarchy depth (defaulting to 100) that can be adjusted by a system administrator, satisfying the practical constraint of Requirement B while preserving the extensibility intent of Requirement A.

Original_FR: The system shall allow physician offices to use social security numbers or medical record numbers to identify patients.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall allow physician offices to only use social security numbers to identify patients.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement B to align with Requirement A by updating it to state that physician offices may use **either** social security numbers **or** medical record numbers as patient identifiers, eliminating the restrictive "only" clause.

2. Clarify the intended business rule with stakeholders to determine whether restricting identification to social security numbers only is a deliberate policy decision (e.g., for compliance or interoperability reasons), and if so, remove Requirement A's reference to medical record numbers to make both requirements consistent.

3. Introduce a configurable system setting that allows physician offices to choose their preferred patient identification method (SSN, MRN, or both), then rewrite both requirements as a single unified requirement reflecting this flexibility, removing the contradiction entirely.

Original_FR: The system shall allow several orders to be placed with the ability for a user to sign for them all at once.
Conflicting_FR: The system must allow several orders to be placed with the ability for a user to sign for them all at once.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, the conflict is minimal — they express the same functional need but differ only in modal verb ("shall" vs. "must") and phrasing ("allow" vs. "allow"). The resolution focuses on standardization and deduplication:

1. **Merge into a single requirement** by selecting one authoritative modal verb per your project's requirements standard (e.g., IEEE 29148 recommends "shall" for mandatory requirements) and removing the duplicate: *"The system shall allow several orders to be placed with the ability for a user to sign for them all at once."*

2. **Establish a modal verb convention** in your requirements style guide to prevent future ambiguity — define "shall" as mandatory, "should" as recommended, and "may" as optional — then update all existing requirements to comply, eliminating the "must" variant.

3. **Review the source of both requirements** to determine if they originated from different stakeholders or documents; if so, consolidate them into a single traceability entry referencing both sources, ensuring all stakeholders acknowledge the unified requirement to avoid re-introduction of the duplicate.

Original_FR: The system shall allow user classes to be used across VistA to replace and/or complement security keys.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall not allow user classes to be used across VistaA to replace and/or complement security keys.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended scope and context by engaging stakeholders to determine whether user classes should replace/complement security keys in specific modules or roles only, then rewrite both requirements with precise conditions (e.g., "The system shall allow user classes to complement, but not fully replace, security keys in non-critical modules, while prohibiting their use as replacements in high-security contexts").

2. Introduce a tiered authorization model that reconciles both requirements: user classes may be used to complement security keys (additive permission layer) but shall never be used to fully replace security keys, effectively preserving the intent of Requirement B's restriction while honoring the flexibility intent of Requirement A.

3. Treat one requirement as superseding the other by formally reviewing the requirements with the product owner and security team to determine which reflects current policy, deprecating the outdated requirement, and documenting the rationale (e.g., if security policy mandates keys remain mandatory, retire Requirement A and update Requirement B to explicitly state the approved use cases where user classes may only complement, never replace, security keys).

Original_FR: The system shall allow users to enter time in several different formats.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall require times to be entered as a 24-hour time, not with a 12-hour clock and an a.m. and p.m.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "several different formats" in Requirement A to explicitly exclude 12-hour (AM/PM) formats, allowing only variations of 24-hour time (e.g., HH:MM, HH:MM:SS, HHMM) to satisfy both requirements simultaneously.

2. Revise Requirement A to specify that the system accepts multiple 24-hour time formats (e.g., with or without colons, with or without seconds), making it consistent with Requirement B while still providing input flexibility to users.

3. If 12-hour AM/PM input is genuinely needed for usability, retain Requirement A as-is and remove or relax Requirement B, replacing it with a requirement that the system stores and displays all times internally in 24-hour format, regardless of the input format used.

Original_FR: The system shall allow users who hold any of the ORES/ORELSE/PROVIDER keys to be viewed as a clinical user and has full access privileges to all problem list options.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall only allow clinical users to have full access privileges to all problem list options.
Resolution:
1. Redefine the "clinical user" role to explicitly include holders of ORES/ORELSE/PROVIDER keys, establishing a clear and unified definition that satisfies both requirements (e.g., "A clinical user is defined as any user holding an ORES, ORELSE, or PROVIDER key, and only clinical users shall have full access privileges to all problem list options").

2. Introduce a role-based access control (RBAC) model where ORES/ORELSE/PROVIDER key holders are automatically mapped to the "clinical user" role upon authentication, ensuring Requirement B's restriction is enforced while Requirement A's access grant is preserved through role assignment rather than direct key-based access.

3. Revise Requirement A to remove the implicit granting of "full access privileges" and instead state that ORES/ORELSE/PROVIDER key holders are *eligible* to be designated as clinical users, with Requirement B remaining the single authoritative rule governing full access — thereby separating user classification criteria from access privilege rules and eliminating the overlap.

Original_FR: The system shall check ICD and CPT code validity as of a specified date when codes are entered, when a new code set is implemented, and whenever CACs or IRM personnel choose to run the option.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall only check ICD code validity as of a specified date when codes are entered, when a new code set is implemented, and whenever CACs or IRM personnel choose to run the option.
Resolution:
1. Merge the requirements into a single unified requirement that explicitly covers both ICD and CPT code validity checks, clarifying that the system shall check **both** ICD and CPT code validity under the specified triggering conditions, thereby eliminating the contradiction introduced by Requirement B's restrictive "only ICD" scope.

2. Clarify stakeholder intent by convening a review session with CACs and IRM personnel to determine whether CPT code validity checking is a required capability; if confirmed, retain Requirement A and retire Requirement B; if CPT checking is out of scope, update Requirement A to match Requirement B's narrower scope and document the rationale for exclusion.

3. If both code types need to be addressed but with different rules or ownership, split the requirements into separate, non-conflicting statements (e.g., one for ICD validity checks and one for CPT validity checks), each with its own clearly defined triggering conditions, so there is no overlap or contradiction between them.

Original_FR: The system shall delete history of received immunizations.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall only delete history of recieved immunizations unless user specifies
Resolution:
1. **Revise Requirement A** to align with Requirement B by updating it to state: "The system shall delete history of received immunizations only when explicitly requested by the user," making user authorization a mandatory precondition for deletion.

2. **Merge both requirements** into a single, unified requirement: "The system shall retain history of received immunizations by default and shall only delete such history upon explicit user request," eliminating the contradiction by clearly establishing the default behavior and the condition for deletion.

3. **Clarify scope and roles** by splitting the requirements: define Requirement A as a system-level administrative capability (e.g., bulk deletion by an admin) and Requirement B as the user-facing behavior, ensuring both can coexist with clearly defined contexts and access control rules.

Original_FR: The system shall display a box listing possible matches if CPRS finds more than one patient with the same last name and same last four digits of the social security numbers.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall display a message of possible matches if more than one match is found when a user partially enters a patient's name and last four digits of a social security number.
Resolution:
Looking at these two requirements, the conflicts involve: (1) the UI element used ("box" vs. "message"), (2) the trigger condition ("same last name and same last four digits" vs. "partial name entry and last four digits"), and (3) implicit differences in search behavior (exact last name match vs. partial name match).

1. **Unify the UI display component** by specifying a single, consistent interface element (e.g., a "match results box/dialog") that is used in all multiple-match scenarios, replacing the ambiguous distinction between "box" and "message" across both requirements.

2. **Consolidate the trigger conditions** into one unified requirement that covers both exact and partial name matches combined with last four SSN digits, explicitly stating that the same display mechanism is triggered whenever more than one patient match is found, regardless of whether the name entry is partial or complete.

3. **Separate the requirements by search mode** if both behaviors are intentionally distinct — clearly define Requirement A as governing exact last-name searches and Requirement B as governing partial-name searches, while standardizing the UI response (same display format and wording) to eliminate the "box" vs. "message" inconsistency and ensure a predictable user experience across both modes.

Original_FR: The System shall generate Inventory Quantity Adjustment document based on product daily sales data and product formulas.
Conflicting_FR: If the inventory adjustment is initiated manually, the System shall generate the Inventory Quantity Adjustment document based on user-input scrap and loss values from the daily production log.
Resolution:
The core conflict here is that **Requirement A** implies the system *automatically* generates the Inventory Quantity Adjustment document using sales data and product formulas, while **Requirement B** implies a *manual* trigger where the document is generated using user-input scrap/loss values from the production log — creating ambiguity about the trigger mechanism and the data source used for document generation.

---

1. **Introduce a trigger-mode distinction:** Refactor both requirements to explicitly define two operational modes — "Automatic Mode" (system-triggered, using daily sales data and product formulas) and "Manual Mode" (user-triggered, using scrap/loss values from the daily production log) — and specify that the system shall support both modes, generating the Inventory Quantity Adjustment document according to the inputs and rules of the active mode.

2. **Unify the document generation rule with conditional logic:** Merge the two requirements into a single requirement that states: *"The System shall generate the Inventory Quantity Adjustment document using daily sales data and product formulas by default; however, if the adjustment is initiated manually, the system shall use user-input scrap and loss values from the daily production log instead."* This eliminates ambiguity by establishing a clear precedence rule.

3. **Clarify and separate data sources from the trigger mechanism:** Explicitly define that the *trigger* (automatic vs. manual) and the *data source* (sales data/formulas vs. scrap/loss values) are two independent dimensions, and require that both data sources be reconciled or auditable in the final document — ensuring traceability regardless of how the adjustment was initiated.

Original_FR: The system shall improve efficiency of entering orders.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall improve efficiency of entering orders by atleast 20 percent.
Resolution:
1. Replace Requirement A with Requirement B, as the more specific quantified version (≥20% improvement) fully encompasses the intent of the vague version, eliminating redundancy while providing a measurable acceptance criterion.

2. Retain Requirement B as the binding requirement and mark Requirement A as deprecated or remove it from the specification to avoid ambiguity during implementation and testing.

3. If stakeholders disagree on the 20% threshold, facilitate a review session to validate the target based on baseline data, then update Requirement A to align with the agreed measurable benchmark, consolidating both into a single, precise requirement.

Original_FR: The system shall include Standard Laboratory and Medication orderable files.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall include non-Standard Laboratory and Medication orderable files.
Resolution:
1. Merge both requirements into a single, unified requirement that explicitly states the system shall support **both Standard and non-Standard** Laboratory and Medication orderable files, eliminating the apparent contradiction by treating them as complementary rather than competing needs.

2. Clarify scope and context by defining precise criteria that distinguish "Standard" from "non-Standard" orderable files (e.g., regulatory classification, institutional approval status), then specify separate modules or configuration settings to manage each category independently within the system.

3. Introduce a tiered or configurable architecture requirement where Standard orderable files are included by default and non-Standard orderable files are supported as an optional, role-restricted extension — ensuring baseline compliance while accommodating flexibility for specialized use cases.

Original_FR: The system shall indicate that the record is sensitive and may only be viewed by authorized users.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall indicate that a record is sensitive and may only be viewed by authorized users only if the user is not authorized.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the display scope by specifying that the sensitivity indicator is always visible to all users (satisfying Requirement A), but access to the actual record content is restricted to authorized users only, separating the indicator behavior from the access control behavior.

2. Revise the requirements to define two distinct behaviors: (a) the system shall always display a sensitivity label on the record regardless of authorization status, and (b) the system shall display an explicit "access denied" or "unauthorized" message only when an unauthorized user attempts to view the record, reconciling both intents.

3. Prioritize Requirement A as the baseline and amend Requirement B to clarify its intent as an additional access-denial notification — specifying that unauthorized users receive a prominent warning message when attempting to access a sensitive record, while the sensitivity indicator remains visible to all users at all times.

Original_FR: The system shall locate the preferred repair facility with the highest ratings for the input criteria
Conflicting_FR: "The system shall locate the preferred repair facility using only the pre-configured default criteria, ignoring any user-inputted rating preferences."
Resolution:
1. Introduce a priority hierarchy: Allow user-inputted criteria to override the pre-configured defaults when provided, but fall back to the default criteria when no user input is given. This reconciles both requirements by making defaults a fallback rather than a fixed rule.

2. Separate the two behaviors into distinct system modes: Define a "default mode" that uses pre-configured criteria and a "custom mode" that accepts user-inputted rating preferences, giving users or administrators the ability to explicitly select which mode applies in a given context.

3. Clarify and align stakeholder intent: Conduct a requirements review with stakeholders to determine whether user-inputted preferences should be supported at all.
